instruction: What is the purpose and scope of Section 372 of the Income-Tax Act?
context:
[Section 372 — Exclusion of time taken for copy]
Chapter: APPEALS, REVISIONS AND ALTERNATE DISPUTE RESOLUTIONS

372. 		In computing the period of limitation prescribed for an appeal or an
							application under this Act, the day on which the order complained of was
served and, if the assessee was not provided with a copy of the order when the notice
of the order was served, the time required to obtain a copy of such order, shall be
excluded.
Filing of appeal by income-tax authority.
gold_response:
Section 372 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, titled 'Exclusion of time taken for copy', falls under APPEALS, REVISIONS AND ALTERNATE DISPUTE RESOLUTIONS.

372. 		In computing the period of limitation prescribed for an appeal or an
							application under this Act, the day on which the order complained of was
served and, if the assessee was not provided with a copy of the order when the notice
of the order was served, the time required to obtain a copy of such order, shall be
excluded.
Filing of appeal by income-tax authority.
model_response:
The purpose of Section 372 of the Income-Tax Act is to exclude from the computation of the period of limitation for filing an appeal against an order made under the Act, any time that may have been spent on obtaining a copy of the order when it was served.

The scope of Section 372 is limited to cases where the assessee did not receive a copy of the order when the notice of the order was served. This means that even if the assessee took some time to gather information or seek clarification after receiving the original order, the delay does not affect the computation of the period of limitation for filing the appeal.

instruction: Explain Section 257 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025.
context:
[Section 257 — Proceedings before income-tax authorities to be judicial proceedings]
Chapter: TAX ADMINISTRATION

257. (1) Any proceeding under this Act before an income-tax authority shall be
					deemed to be a judicial proceeding within the meaning of sections 229 and
267 and for the purposes of section 233 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
(45 of 2023).
(2) Every income-tax authority shall be deemed to be a Civil Court for the purposes
of section 215 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (46 of 2023),
but not for the purposes of Chapter XXVIII of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha
Sanhita, 2023 (46 of 2023).
Disclosure of information relating to assessees.
gold_response:
Section 257 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, titled 'Proceedings before income-tax authorities to be judicial proceedings', falls under TAX ADMINISTRATION.

257. (1) Any proceeding under this Act before an income-tax authority shall be
					deemed to be a judicial proceeding within the meaning of sections 229 and
267 and for the purposes of section 233 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023
(45 of 2023).
(2) Every income-tax authority shall be deemed to be a Civil Court for the purposes
of section 215 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (46 of 2023),
but not for the purposes of Chapter XXVIII of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha
Sanhita, 2023 (46 of 2023).
Disclosure of information relating to assessees.
model_response:
Section 257 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 is as follows:

"Any proceeding under this Act before an income-tax authority shall be deemed to be a judicial proceeding within the meaning of sections 229 and 267 and for the purposes of section 233 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023).

Explanation - For the purposes of this section, a proceeding means any proceeding that involves the assessment or collection of tax."

This section essentially clarifies that when there is a dispute between two parties related to taxation, such as an assessee and an income-tax authority, the case will be treated as a judicial proceeding. This provision aims to ensure that disputes related to taxation are handled in a fair and transparent manner, similar to how civil court cases are handled.

instruction: Summarise the key provisions of Section 426 (Interest on excess refund).
context:
[Section 426 — Interest on excess refund]
Chapter: APPEALS, REVISIONS AND ALTERNATE DISPUTE RESOLUTIONS

426.		(1) Subject to the other provisions of this Act, where any refund is
						granted to the assessee under section 270(1), and—
         (a) no refund is due on regular assessment; or
         (b) the amount refunded under section 270(1) exceeds the amount
             refundable on regular assessment,
the assessee shall be liable to pay simple interest at the rate of 0.5% on the whole
or the excess amount so refunded, for every month or part of a month comprised in
the period from the date of grant of refund to the date of such regular assessment.
(2) Where, as a result of an order under section 287 or 288 or 359 or 363 or 365(10)
or 368 or 377 or 378, the amount of refund granted under section 270(1) is held to
be correctly allowed, either in whole or in part, then, the interest chargeable, if any,
under sub-section (1) shall be reduced accordingly.
(3) Where in relation to a tax year, an assessment is made for the first time under
section 279, the assessment so made shall be regarded as a regular assessment for
the purposes of this section.
gold_response:
Section 426 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, titled 'Interest on excess refund', falls under APPEALS, REVISIONS AND ALTERNATE DISPUTE RESOLUTIONS.

426.		(1) Subject to the other provisions of this Act, where any refund is
						granted to the assessee under section 270(1), and—
         (a) no refund is due on regular assessment; or
         (b) the amount refunded under section 270(1) exceeds the amount
             refundable on regular assessment,
the assessee shall be liable to pay simple interest at the rate of 0.5% on the whole
or the excess amount so refunded, for every month or part of a month comprised in
the period from the date of grant of refund to the date of such regular assessment.
(2) Where, as a result of an order under section 287 or 288 or 359 or 363 or 365(10)
or 368 or 377 or 378, the amount of refund granted under section 270(1) is held to
be correctly allowed, either in whole or in part, then, the interest chargeable, if any,
under sub-section (1) shall be reduced accordingly.
(3) Where in relation to a tax year, an assessment is made for the first time under
section 279, the assessment so made shall be regarded as a regular assessment for
the purposes of this section.
model_response:
According to Section 426 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026), when a refund exceeds the amount that would have been payable on a regular assessment, the assessee is liable to pay simple interest on the excess amount for the entire period from the date of grant of refund to the date of the regular assessment.

In simpler terms, if the refund exceeds the expected amount, the assessee must pay interest on the excess amount for the full duration of the tax year.

instruction: Explain Section 91 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025.
context:
[Section 91 — Reference to Valuation Officer]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

91. (1) For ascertaining the fair market value of a capital asset for this
		 Chapter, the Assessing Officer may refer the valuation of the capital asset to a
Valuation Officer,—
       (a) if the value of the asset claimed by the assessee is as per the estimate by
           a registered valuer, but the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that the
           value so claimed is at variance with its fair market value;
       (b) in any other case, if the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that—
             (i) the fair market value of the asset exceeds the value claimed by
                 the assessee by more than the percentage of value of such asset or
                 amount, as may be prescribed; or
            (ii) having regard to the nature of the asset and other relevant circum-
                 stances, it is necessary so to do.
(2) The provisions of section 269(3) to (8) shall, with necessary modifications,
apply in relation to such reference made under sub-section (1).
                           F.—Income from other sources
Income from other sources.
gold_response:
Section 91 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, titled 'Reference to Valuation Officer', falls under COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME.

91. (1) For ascertaining the fair market value of a capital asset for this
		 Chapter, the Assessing Officer may refer the valuation of the capital asset to a
Valuation Officer,—
       (a) if the value of the asset claimed by the assessee is as per the estimate by
           a registered valuer, but the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that the
           value so claimed is at variance with its fair market value;
       (b) in any other case, if the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that—
             (i) the fair market value of the asset exceeds the value claimed by
                 the assessee by more than the percentage of value of such asset or
                 amount, as may be prescribed; or
            (ii) having regard to the nature of the asset and other relevant circum-
                 stances, it is necessary so to do.
(2) The provisions of section 269(3) to (8) shall, with necessary modifications,
apply in relation to such reference made under sub-section (1).
                           F.—Income from other sources
Income from other sources.
model_response:
Section 91 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 reads as follows:
"(1) For ascertaining the fair market value of a capital asset for this Chapter, the Assessing Officer may refer the valuation of the capital asset to a Valuation Officer,—
(a) if the value of the asset claimed by the assessee is as per the estimate by a registered valuer, but the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that the value so claimed is at variance with its fair market value;
(b) in any other case, if the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that—
(i) the fair market value of the asset exceeds the value claimed by the assessee by more than the percentage of value of such asset or amount, as may be prescribed; or
(ii) having regard to the nature of the asset and other relevant circumstances, it is necessary so to do."

Explanation: This section deals with the process of referring a capital asset's valuation to a Valuation Officer.

Step-by-step explanation:

The Assessing Officer has the authority to refer a capital asset's valuation to a Valuation Officer if there is a discrepancy between the estimated value provided by a registered valuer and the actual value claimed by the assessee.

In this scenario, the Assessor may decide to refer the valuation of the asset to a Valuation Officer due to the following reasons:

(a) If the estimated value provided by a registered valuer does not match the actual value claimed by the assessee.

This can happen when the estimated value is lower than the actual value, which is acceptable according to the regulations.

(b) In cases where the estimated value exceeds the prescribed percentage of the actual value, the Assessor may decide to refer the valuation to a Valuation Officer.

This can occur when the estimated value is significantly higher than the actual value, indicating that the assessed value is not reasonable.

(c) In all other cases, the Assessor may decide to refer the valuation to a Valuation Officer, considering factors like the nature of the asset and other relevant circumstances.

Therefore, the purpose of this section is to ensure that the Assessor accurately determines the fair market value of a capital asset, taking into account various factors and providing an accurate assessment.

instruction: Explain how Section 248 affects the application of Section 294.
context:
[Section 294 — Procedure for block assessment]
Chapter: PROCEDURE FOR ASSESSMENT

294.		(1) Where any search has been62 initiated or requisition is made in the case of
					 any person, then,—
       (a) the Assessing Officer shall, in respect of such search or requisition,
           issue a notice to such person, requiring him to furnish within a period
           specified in the notice, not exceeding sixty days, a return in the form
           and verified in the manner, as may be prescribed, setting forth his
           undisclosed income, for the block period, and—
             (i) such return shall be considered as if it was a return furnished
                 under section 263 and thereafter notice under section 270(8) shall
                 be issued;
            (ii) any return furnished beyond the period allowed in the notice shall
                 not be deemed to be a return under section 263;
           (iii) no notice under section 280 is required to be issued for the purpose
                 of proceeding under this part;
           (iv) a person who has furnished a return under this clause shall not be
                entitled to furnish a revised return;
            (v) the time allowed for furnishing a return under this clause may be
                extended by a further period of thirty days, where—
                 (A) in respect of a tax year immediately preceding the tax year in
                     which the search is initiated or requisition is made, the due
                     date for furnishing the return has not expired prior to the date
                     of initiation of such search or requisition;
                 (B) the assessee was liable for audit under section 63 for such tax
                     year;
                 (C) the accounts (maintained in normal course) of such tax year
                     have not been audited on the date of issuance of such notice;
                     and
                 (D) the assessee requests in writing for extension of time for
                     furnishing such return to get such accounts audited;
      (b) the Assessing Officer shall proceed to determine the total undisclosed
          income of the block period in the manner laid down in section 293 and
          the provisions of sections 268, 270(8), 270(10), 271, 276, 277 and 278
          shall, so far as may be, apply;
       (c) the Assessing Officer, on determination of the total undisclosed income
           of the block period in accordance with this part, shall pass an order of
           assessment or reassessment and determine the tax payable by him on the
           basis of such assessment or reassessment, and the provisions of section
           275 shall not apply in respect of such order;
      (d) the assets seized under section 247 or requisitioned under section 248
          shall be dealt with as per section 250.
(2) The provisions of section 270(1) shall not apply to the return furnished under
this section.
(3) The Assessing Officer, before issuance of notice under sub-section (1)(a), shall
take prior approval of the Additional Commissioner or the Additional Director or
the Joint Commissioner or the Joint Director.
Undisclosed income of any other person.

            (v) the time allowed for furnishing a return under this clause may be
         

Readers should also refer to Section 248 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 294 deals with the procedure for assessing block assessments. According to Section 294, when a search is initiated or requisition is made in relation to a person, the Assessing Officer must issue a notice to that person requiring them to furnish a return within a specified period.

Now, let's analyze how Section 294 affects the application of Section 248.

Section 248 specifically deals with the seizure of assets and requisitioning of property. It provides that the assets seized under Section 247 or requisitioned under Section 248 shall be dealt with accordingly.

In the context of Section 294, Section 248 essentially sets out the procedures for dealing with seized assets. When a search is initiated or requisition is made, Section 248 ensures that the assets are handled in a specific way.

Here's how Section 294 affects the application of Section 248:

*   **Requisition vs Seizure**: In Section 294, the term "requisition" refers to the process of issuing a notice to a person about a search or requisition. This implies that the assets are being requisitioned, whereas in Section 248, the term "seize" refers to the actual act of taking possession of the assets.
*   **Notice Period**: The fact that a notice is issued under Section 294 does not necessarily mean that the assets will be seized under Section 248. The notice period mentioned in Section 294 allows for a certain timeframe for the person to respond to the notice, but it does not imply that the assets are seized until after that period.
*   **Dealing with Seized Assets**: Once the assets are seized under Section 247 or requisitioned under Section 248, they are dealt with according to the procedures outlined in those sections. However, since Section 294 only mentions the issuance of a notice, it does not directly affect the handling of seized assets.
*   **Extension of Time**: If the due date for furnishing a return under Section 294 is extended, it would impact the overall process, including the handling of seized assets. However, the extension of time is not explicitly mentioned in Section 294, suggesting that it might not directly affect the seizure of assets.

In summary, while Section 294 initiates the process of assessing block assessments, it does not directly affect the application of Section 248, which deals with the seizure and handling of assets. The two sections operate independently, with Section 294 focusing on the procedural aspects of assessing block assessments and Section 248 detailing the procedures

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 148 (Deduction in respect of certain inter-corporate dividends) and Section 263?
context:
[Section 148 — Deduction in respect of certain inter-corporate dividends]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

148.		(1) If the gross total income of a domestic company in any tax year includes
					any income by way of dividends from—
       (a) any other domestic company; or
       (b) a foreign company; or
       (c) a business trust,
such domestic company shall be allowed a deduction of an amount equal to so much
of the income by way of dividends received from the person mentioned in clause
(a) or (b) or (c) as does not exceed the amount of dividend distributed by it at least
one month before the due date for filing the return of income under section 263(1).
(2) Where any deduction, in respect of the amount of dividend distributed by the
domestic company, has been allowed under sub-section (1) in any tax year, no
deduction shall be allowed in respect of such amount in any other tax year.
Deduction in respect of income of co-operative societies.
gold_response:
Section 148 (Deduction in respect of certain inter-corporate dividends) refers to Section 263 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

148.		(1) If the gross total income of a domestic company in any tax year includes
					any income by way of dividends from—
       (a) any other domestic company; or
       (b) a foreign company; or
       (c) a business trust,
such domestic company shall be allowed a deduction of an amount equal to so much
of the income by way of dividends received from the person mentioned in clause
(a) or (b) or (c) as does not exceed the amount of dividend distributed by it at least
one month before the due date for filing the return of income under section 263(1).
(2) Where any deduction, in respect of the amount of dividend distributed by the
domestic company, has been allowed under sub-section (1) in any tax year, no
deduction shall be allowed in respect of such amount in any other tax year.
Deduction in respect of income of co-operative societies.

Readers should also refer to Section 263 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 148 and 263 is that they deal with the same concept - "income by way of dividends". 

Section 148 allows a deduction of an amount equal to the excess of dividend distributed by a domestic company over the minimum amount of dividend distributed by it, if the gross total income of the domestic company exceeds a certain threshold.

Section 263, however, deals with the "return of income" which is a separate process where a company files its return of income after deducting all allowable deductions. It does not directly relate to the concept of "dividend distribution".

Therefore, while both sections deal with the concept of "dividends", they operate independently of each other.

instruction:
What is the relationship between Section 88 (Irrespective of anything contained in section 87, if the assessee has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfe) and Section 87?
context:
[Section 88 — Irrespective of anything contained in section 87, if the assessee has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfe]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

88. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 87, if the assessee has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfer of a capital asset, being machin-
           ery or plant or building or land or any rights in building or land used
           for the business of an industrial undertaking situated in an urban area,
           effected in the course of or in consequence of shifting of such industrial
           undertaking (original asset) to any Special Economic Zone in any urban
           or any other area; and
       (b) has within one year before or three years after the date of such transfer,—
             (i) purchased machinery or plant for the business of the industrial
                 undertaking in such Special Economic Zone;
            (ii) acquired building or land or constructed building for his business
                 in such Special Economic Zone;
           (iii) shifted the original asset and transferred the establishment of such
                 undertaking to such Special Economic Zone; and
            (iv) incurred expenses on such other purposes specified by a scheme
                 notified by the Central Government in this behalf,
then, instead of capital gain being charged to income-tax as income of the tax year
in which the transfer took place, it shall be dealt with as follows:—
      (A) if the cost and expenses incurred in on all or any of the purposes men-
          tioned sub-clauses (i) to (iv) referred to as “new asset”,—
             (I) is less than the capital gains, the difference shall be charged under
                 section 67 as the income of the tax year; or
            (II) is equal to or more than the capital gains, no capital gain shall be
                 charged under section 67;
      (B) for computing any capital gain arising from transfer of the new asset
          within three years of its being purchased, acquired, constructed or
          transferred, the cost shall be nil in case of sub-clause (A)(II), or shall be
          reduced by the amount of the capital gain in case of sub-clause (A)(I).
(2) If the capital gain referred to in sub-section (1) is not utilised by the assessee
for the new asset within one year before the transfer of the original asset, or before
filing the return of income under section 263, then,—
       (a) the unutilised amount shall be deposited in a specified bank or institution
           and utilised as per the scheme notified by the Central Government;
       (b) such deposit shall be made before the filing of the return and not later
           than the due date applicable in the case of the assessee for filing the
           return of income under section 263(1); and
       (c) the proof of deposit shall be submitted along with such return.
(3) For the purposes of sub-section (1), the amount already utilised for purchasing
or constructing the new asset together with the deposited amount under sub-section
(2) shall be deemed to be the cost of the new asset.
(4) If the amount deposited under sub-section (2) is not wholly or partly utilised for
the new asset within the period specified in sub-section (1), then,—
       (a) the unutilised amount shall be charged under section 67 as the income
           of the tax year in which the period of three years from the date of the
           transfer of the original asset expires; and
       (b) the assessee shall be entitled to withdraw such unutilised amount in
           accordance with the scheme referred to in sub-section (2).
(5) For the purpose of this section, the expression “urban area” shall have the mean-
ing assigned to it in section 87.

of capital gains.
gold_response:
Section 88 (Irrespective of anything contained in section 87, if the assessee has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfe) refers to Section 87 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

88. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 87, if the assessee has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfer of a capital asset, being machin-

           for the business of an industrial undertaking situated in an urban area,
           effected in the course of or in consequence of shifting of such industrial
           undertaking (original asset) to any Special Economic Zone in any urban
           or any other area; and
       (b) has within one year before or three years after the date of such transfer,—
             (i) purchased machinery or plant for the business of the industrial
                 undertaking in such Special Economic Zone;
            (ii) acquired building or land or constructed building for his business
                 in such Special Economic Zone;
           (iii) shifted the original asset and transferred the establishment of such
                 undertaking to such Special Economic Zone; and
            (iv) incurred expenses on such other purposes specified by a scheme
                 notified by the Central Government in this behalf,
then, ins

Readers should also refer to Section 87 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 88 and 87 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 is that they deal with the same concept but differ in their scope and application.

Section 87 deals with the definition of "capital gains" and provides that capital gains arise when there is a change in ownership of property, including real estate, shares, and securities.

On the other hand, Section 88 specifically addresses the situation where the capital gains arise from the transfer of a capital asset, such as machinery, plants, buildings, or land, resulting in the shift of an industrial undertaking to a Special Economic Zone (SEZ).

In essence, Section 87 defines capital gains, while Section 88 describes how these gains are calculated and taxed. The key difference lies in the fact that Section 87 does not require the transfer of assets to result in a change in ownership, whereas Section 88 requires the transfer of assets to result in a change in ownership.

Therefore, Section 88 is a specific provision that applies only to cases where there is a transfer of a capital asset, whereas Section 87 is a broader definition of capital gains that can apply to various types of transactions.

model_response:
Section 402 of the Income Tax Act, 2013 (17 of 2013) defines "brokerage" and provides that it includes any agreement or understanding or action in concert between two or more persons for the sale or purchase of goods or property.

Therefore, according to Section 402, if a person is a promoter, he is considered to be involved in a "brokerage" activity even if his shares are listed on a recognized stock exchange in India. This means that the promoter's income would be included in the computation of total income under Section 66.

However, if the promoter does not hold more than 10% of the shareholding in the company, then the definition of "brokerage" would only apply to him when he holds less than 10% of the shareholding in the company. In such cases, the promoter's income would not be included in the computation of total income under Section 66.

instruction: Explain how Section 263 affects the application of Section 154.
context:
[Section 154 — Deduction in case of a person with disability]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

154.			(1) An individual, being resident in India, who is certified by a medical
							authority, at any time during the tax year, as a person with disability or
person with severe disability, shall be allowed a deduction of ` 75000 or ` 125000,
respectively, while computing his total income.
(2) The deduction under sub-section (1) shall be allowed only if all of the following
conditions are fulfilled:—
       (a) the individual furnishes a copy of the certificate issued by the medical
           authority;
       (b) if the certificate specifies that the disability needs reassessment of its
           extent after a period stipulated in it, the deduction shall not be allowed
           for any tax year succeeding the tax year in which the certificate expires,
           unless a new disability certificate is obtained and furnished; and
       (c) the certificate referred to in clauses (a) and (b) of this sub-section is
           furnished in the form and manner, as may be prescribed, along with the
           return of income under section 263 for the tax year in which the deduc-
           tion is claimed.
(3) For the purposes of this section, “disability”, “medical authority”, “person with
disability” or “person with severe disability” shall have the same meanings as pro-
vided in section 127.
gold_response:
Section 154 (Deduction in case of a person with disability) refers to Section 263 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

154.			(1) An individual, being resident in India, who is certified by a medical
							authority, at any time during the tax year, as a person with disability or
person with severe disability, shall be allowed a deduction of ` 75000 or ` 125000,
respectively, while computing his total income.
(2) The deduction under sub-section (1) shall be allowed only if all of the following
conditions are fulfilled:—
       (a) the individual furnishes a copy of the certificate issued by the medical
           authority;
       (b) if the certificate specifies that the disability needs reassessment of its
           extent after a period stipulated in it, the deduction shall not be allowed
           for any tax year succeeding the tax year in which the certificate expires,
           unless a new disability certificate is obtained and furnished; and
       (c) the certificate referred to in clauses (a) and (b) of this sub-section is
           furnished in the form and manner, as may be prescribed, along with the
           return of income under section 263 for the tax year in which the deduc-
           tion is claimed.

Readers should also refer to Section 263 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 263 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) deals with the filing of returns for the financial year. It provides for the payment of tax on certain types of income, including interest income, dividend income, etc.

Now, considering the given provision, we can see that Section 263 allows individuals to claim deductions up to a maximum amount of ₹75,000 or ₹125,000, respectively, depending on whether they are classified as a person with disability or a person with severe disability.

However, there is no direct relation between Section 263 and Section 154. Section 263 is concerned with the filing of returns and payment of tax, whereas Section 154 is related to the deduction of ₹75,000 or ₹125,000 for individuals with disabilities.

To clarify, Section 263 requires individuals to file their returns and pay tax on specified types of income, but it does not provide for the deduction of ₹75,000 or ₹125,000. On the other hand, Section 154 specifically deals with the deduction of ₹75,000 or ₹125,000 for individuals with disabilities.

Therefore, Section 263 has no effect on the application of Section 154.

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 341 (Application of income) and Section 263?
context:
[Section 341 — Application of income]
Chapter: PROCEDURE FOR ASSESSMENT

341.		(1) The following sums shall be allowed as application of income to a
					 registered non-profit organisation:—
       (a) any sum, other than the sum referred to in clause (b), applied by it for
           charitable or religious purpose in India for which it is registered where
           such sum is paid during the tax year provided that the provisions of
           section 35(b)(i) and section 36(4), (5), (6) and (7) shall apply in respect
           of such sum; and
       (b) 85% of the sum paid by way of donation made to any other registered
           non-profit organisation.
(2) The application of income under sub-section (1) shall include the following:—
       (a) the amount invested or deposited back during the tax year, in the modes
           permitted under section 350 maintained specifically for such corpus, if—
             (i) such investment or depositing back is made within five years from
                 the end of the tax year in which such application of income was
                 made from the corpus; and
            (ii) the application of income from the corpus is made after the 31st
                 March, 2021 and there was no violation of any provision of this
                 Part, or any corresponding provision of the Income-tax Act, 1961
                 (43 of 1961) with respect to such application;
       (b) the amount repaid, during the tax year, towards any loan or borrowing
           where,—
             (i) such repayment is within five years from the end of the tax year
                 in which such application of income was made from the loan or
                 borrowing; and
            (ii) the application of income from the loan or borrowing is made after
                 the 31st March, 2021 and there was no violation of any provision
                 of this Part, or any corresponding provision of the Income-tax Act,
                 1961 (43 of 1961) with respect to such application.
(3) The following claims shall not be allowed as application of income under
sub-sections (1) and (2):—
       (a) the deduction or allowance by way of depreciation or otherwise claimed
           in respect of an asset acquisition of which has been claimed as an ap-
           plication of income in the same or any other tax year under this Part or
           under any corresponding provision of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of
           1961); or
       (b) a claim of set off or deduction or allowance of any excess application of
           any of the years preceding the tax year; or
       (c) any sum paid as a corpus donation to any other registered non-profit
           organisation.
(4) An application from corpus, loan or borrowing, accumulated income, specified
income or deemed accumulated income shall not be considered as application for
the purpose of sub-sections (1) and (2).
(5) Where, in a tax year, the regular income applied by a registered non-profit
organisation towards charitable or religious purposes in India, as per the provisions
of sub-sections (1) to (4), is less than 85% of regular income, the shortfall, or any
part thereof, at the option of the registered non-profit organisation, may be treated
as deemed application.
(6) Any deemed application under sub-section (5) shall be applied by the registered
non-profit organisation for its objects in India,—
       (a) during the tax year in which the income is received or in the tax year
           immediately succeeding such tax year, where such shortfall is for the
           reason that the whole or any part of the income has not been received
           during that tax year;
       (b) in the tax year immediately succeeding the tax year in which the income
           was derived, where such shortfall is for any other reason.
(7) The option under sub-section (5) shall be exercised on or before the due date
specified in section 263(1) for furnishing the return of income for such tax year, in
such form and manner, as may be prescribed.

            (ii) the application of income from the corpus is made after the 31st
                 March, 2021 and there was no violation of any p

Readers should also refer to Section 263 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 341 and 263 is as follows:
- Both sections deal with the application of income of a registered non-profit organization.
- Section 263 deals with the due date for filing returns of income for a tax year.
- Section 341 allows for the application of income of a registered non-profit organization in certain circumstances, while Section 263 sets out the due date for filing returns of income for a tax year.

In essence, Section 263 provides a deadline for filing returns of income for a tax year, whereas Section 341 allows for the application of income of a registered non-profit organization in specific circumstances, such as when the regular income applied by the organization is less than 85% of regular income.

instruction: Explain how Section 263 affects the application of Section 86.
context:
[Section 86 — If an individual or a Hindu undivided family has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfer of any long-term ca]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

86. (1) If an individual or a Hindu undivided family has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfer of any long-term capital asset, not
           being a residential house (original asset); and
       (b) within one year before, or two years after, the date of such transfer,
           purchased, or has within three years after that date constructed, one
           residential house in India (new asset),
then, the capital gains shall be dealt with as follows:—
       (i) if the net consideration is more than the cost of the new asset, so much
           of the capital gains as bears to the whole of the capital gains, the same
           proportion as the cost of the new asset bears to the net consideration,
           shall not be charged under section 67; or
      (ii) if the net consideration is equal to or less than the cost of the new asset,
           no capital gains shall be charged under section 67.
(2) If the net consideration referred to in sub-section (1) is not utilised by the
assessee to purchase the new asset within one year before the date of transfer of the
original asset, or is not utilised for the purchase or construction of the new asset
before filing the return of income under section 263, then,—
       (a) the unutilised amount shall be deposited in a specified bank or institution
           and utilised as per the scheme notified by the Central Government;
       (b) such deposit shall be made before the filing of the return and not later
           than the due date applicable in the case of the assessee for filing the
           return of income under section 263; and
       (c) the proof of deposit shall be submitted along with such return.
(3) For the purposes of sub-section (1), the amount already utilised for purchasing
or constructing the new asset together with the deposited amount under sub-sec-
tion (2) shall, subject to sub-section (8), be deemed to be the cost of the new asset.
(4) If the amount deposited under sub-section (2) is not wholly or partly utilised for
purchasing or constructing the new asset within the period specified in sub-section
(1), then,—
       (a) the amount determined as per the following formula shall be charged
           under section 67 as income of the tax year in which three years from the
           date of the transfer of the original asset expires:—
		 X – Y,
		where,—
		 X = the capital gains not charged under section 67 as per sub-section (1).
		 Y = the capital gains that would not have been charged under section
   67, if the cost of the new asset had been taken to be the amount actually
   utilised for purchase or construction of the new asset;
       (b) the assessee shall be entitled to withdraw such unutilised amount in
           accordance with the scheme referred to in sub-section (2).
(5) The provisions of sub-section (1) shall not apply, if—
       (a) the assessee—
             (i) owns more than one residential house, other than the new asset,
                 on the date of transfer of the original asset; or
            (ii) purchases any residential house, other than the new asset, within
                 one year of transfer of the original asset; or
           (iii) constructs any residential house, other than the new asset, within
                 three years of transfer of the original asset; and
       (b) the income from such residential house, other than the one residential
           house owned on the date of transfer of the original asset, is chargeable
           under the head “Income from house property”.
(6) If the assessee purchases, within two years after the date of transfer of the
original asset, or constructs, within three years after such date, any residential
house, the income from which is chargeable under the head “Income from house
property”, other than the new asset, the capital gains not charged under section
67 on the basis of cost of such new asset as per sub-section (1), shall be charged as
long-term capital gains of the tax year in which such residential house is purchased
or constructed.
gold_response:

       (a) capital gains arising from the transfer of any long-term ca) refers to Section 263 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

86. (1) If an individual or a Hindu undivided family has—
       (a) capital gains arising from the transfer of any long-term capital asset, not

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 134 (Deductions in respect of rents paid) and Section 21?
context:
[Section 134 — Deductions in respect of rents paid]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

134.			(1) In computing the total income of an assessee, subject to other provisions
						of this section, there shall be deducted any expenditure incurred by him
towards payment of rent (by whatever name called) in respect of any furnished or
unfurnished accommodation occupied by him for the purposes of his own residence.
(2) The deduction under sub-section (1) shall be allowable on payment of such rent
exceeding 10% of his total income, subject to a maximum of ` 5000 per month, or
25% of total income for tax year, whichever is less.
(3) For the purposes of deduction under sub-section (1), such other conditions
or limitations having regard to the area or place in which such accommodation
is situated and other relevant consideration, as may be prescribed, shall be taken
into account.
(4) No deduction under this section shall be allowed to an assessee in any case,
where—
      (a) any residential accommodation is—
             (i) owned by the assessee or by his spouse or minor child or, where
                 such assessee is a member of a Hindu undivided family, by such
                 family at the place where he ordinarily resides or performs duties
                 of his office or employment or carries on his business or profession;
                 or
            (ii) owned by the assessee at any other place, being accommodation
                 in the occupation of the assessee, the value of which is to be deter-
                 mined under section 21(6) or (7)(a); or
       (b) the assessee has any income falling in Schedule III (Table: Sl. No. 11).
(5) For the purposes of this section, the expressions “10% of his total income” and
“25% of his total income” shall mean 10% or 25%, as the case may be, of the total
income of the assessee before allowing deduction for any expenditure under this
section.
Deduction in respect of certain donations for scientific research or rural
development.

towards payment of rent (by whatever name called) in respect of any furnished or

exceeding 10% of his total income, subject to a maximum of ` 5000 per month, or

or limitations having regard to the area or place in which such accommodation
is situated and other relevant consideration, as may be prescribed, shall be taken

Readers should also refer to Section 21 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 134 and 21 is that they deal with different aspects of deductions.

Section 134 deals with "Deductions in respect of rents paid" and provides rules for deducting expenses related to renting out property.

Section 21, on the other hand, deals with "Exemptions from income tax". It specifically mentions that if an individual owns residential premises, they can claim exemptions from income tax on their rental income.

In other words, Section 134 allows deductions for rent paid towards renting out property, while Section 21 provides exemptions from income tax for individuals who own residential premises.

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 315 (Assessment after partition of Hindu undivided family) and Section 270?
context:
[Section 315 — Assessment after partition of Hindu undivided family]
Chapter: PROCEDURE FOR ASSESSMENT

315.		(1) A Hindu family, hitherto assessed as undivided, shall be deemed for the
					purposes of this Act to continue to be a Hindu undivided family, except where
and in so far as a finding of partition has been given under this section in respect
of the Hindu undivided family.
(2) Where, at the time of making an assessment under section 270 or section 271, it
is claimed by or on behalf of any member of a Hindu family assessed as undivided
that a partition, whether total or partial, has taken place among the members of
such family, the Assessing Officer shall make an inquiry thereinto after giving notice
of the inquiry to all the members of the family.
(3) On the completion of the inquiry, the Assessing Officer shall record a finding as
to whether there has been a total or partial partition of the joint family property,
and, if there has been such a partition, the date on which it has taken place.
(4) Where a finding of total or partial partition has been recorded by the Assessing
Officer under this section, and the partition took place during the tax year,—
       (a) the total income of the joint family in respect of the period up to the date
           of partition shall be assessed as if no partition had taken place; and
       (b) each member or group of members shall, in addition to any tax for which
           he or it may be separately liable and irrespective of anything contained
           against Schedule III (Table: Sl. No. 1), be jointly and severally liable for
           the tax on the income so assessed.
(5) Where a finding of total or partial partition has been recorded by the Assessing
Officer under this section, and the partition took place after the expiry of the tax
year, the total income of the tax year of the joint family shall be assessed as if no
partition had taken place, and the provisions of sub-section (4)(b), shall so far as
may be, apply to the case.
(6) Irrespective of anything contained in this section, if the Assessing Officer finds
after completion of the assessment of a Hindu undivided family that the family has
already effected a partition, whether total or partial, the Assessing Officer shall pro-
ceed to recover the tax from every person who was a member of the family before
the partition, and every such person shall be jointly and severally liable for the tax
on the income so assessed.
(7) The provisions of this section shall, so far as may be, apply in relation to the levy
and collection of any penalty, interest, fine or other sum in respect of any period up
to the date of the partition, whether total or partial, of a Hindu undivided family as
they apply in relation to the levy and collection of tax in respect of any such period.
(8) Irrespective of anything contained in the foregoing provisions of this section,
where a partial partition has taken place after the 31st December, 1978, among the
members of a Hindu undivided family hitherto assessed as undivided,—
       (a) a claim that such partial partition has taken place shall not be inquired into
           under sub-section (2) and no finding shall be recorded under sub-section
           (3) that such partial partition had taken place and any finding recorded
           under sub-section (3) to that effect at any time, shall be null and void;
       (b) such family shall continue to be liable to be assessed under this Act as
           if no such partial partition had taken place; and
       (c) each member or group of members of such family immediately before
           such partial partition and the family shall be jointly and severally liable
           for any tax, penalty, interest, fine or other sum payable under this Act by
           the family in respect of any period, whether before or after such partial
           partition.
gold_response:
Section 315 (Assessment after partition of Hindu undivided family) refers to Section 270 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

315.		(1) A Hindu family, hitherto assessed as undivided, shall be deemed for the
					purposes of this Act to continue to be a Hindu undivided family, except where
and in so far as a finding of partition has been given under this section in respect
of the Hindu undivided family.
(2) Where, at the time of making an assessment under section 270 or section 271, it
is claimed by or on behalf of any member of a Hindu family assessed as undivided
that a partition, whether total or partial, has taken place among the members of
such family, the Assessing Officer shall make an inquiry thereinto after giving notice
of the inquiry to all the members of the family.
(3) On the completion of the inquiry, the Assessing Officer shall record a finding as
to whether there has been a total or partial partition of the joint family property,
and, if there has been such a partition, the date on which it has taken place.

Readers should also refer to Section 270 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
According to the provided text, Section 315 deals with the assessment of a Hindu undivided family after a partition, while Section 270 deals with the assessment of a Hindu undivided family before a partition.

In essence, Section 315 provides for the assessment of a Hindu undivided family after a partition, whereas Section 270 sets out the procedures for assessing a Hindu undivided family before a partition. In other words, Section 315 addresses the consequences of a partition, i.e., how to assess the family's income after the partition, whereas Section 270 addresses the process of assessing the family before the partition.

model_response:
Section 159 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) deals with the establishment of a new type of fund known as "Category I" or "Category II" or "Category III Alternative Investment Funds". According to this section, a fund can be classified under one or more of these categories if it meets certain conditions.

Now, let's analyze how Section 159 affects the application of Section 5 regarding the definition of "original fund".

According to Section 5, the original fund refers to a fund that is not a person resident in India, nor is it a resident of a country or a specified territory with an agreement entered into or established or registered in such country or territory.

In the context of Section 159, a Category I or Category II or Category III Alternative Investment Fund is considered to be a non-resident Indian (NRI) or non-resident foreign national (NRFN) who is not a resident of India. This is because these types of funds are established outside India, but they are managed and administered in India.

Therefore, according to Section 159, a Category I or Category II or Category III Alternative Investment Fund would meet the condition of being a non-resident of India, making them eligible for classification under Section 5.

This implies that the definition of "original fund" in Section 5 is satisfied when a Category I or Category II or Category III Alternative Investment Fund is established outside India, even if it is managed or administered in India.

instruction: What is the relationship between Section 500 (Provisional attachment to protect revenue in certain cases) and Section 269?
context:
[Section 500 — Provisional attachment to protect revenue in certain cases]
Chapter: MISCELLANEOUS

500.		(1) Where, during the pendency of any proceeding for—
       (a) the assessment of any income or for the assessment or reassessment of
           any income, which has escaped assessment; or
       (b) imposition of penalty under section 444, where the amount or aggregate
           of amounts of penalty likely to be imposed under the said section exceeds
           two crore rupees,
the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that for protecting the interests of the reve-
nue it is necessary so to do, he may, with the previous approval of the Competent
Authority by order in writing, attach provisionally any property belonging to the
assessee in the manner prescribed in section 413.
(2) Every provisional attachment under sub-section (1) shall cease to have effect after
the expiry of six months from the date of the order made under the said sub-section.
(3) The Competent Authority may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, extend the
period referred to in sub-section (2) and the total period of such extension shall not
exceed two years or sixty days after the date of order of assessment or reassessment,
whichever is later.
(4) Where the assessee furnishes a guarantee from a scheduled bank for an amount
not less than the fair market value of the property provisionally attached under
sub-section (1), the Assessing Officer shall, by an order in writing, revoke such
attachment.
(5) For the purposes of sub-section (4), where the Assessing Officer is satisfied that
a guarantee from a scheduled bank for an amount lower than the fair market value
of the property is sufficient to protect the interests of the revenue, he may accept
such guarantee and revoke the attachment.
(6) The Assessing Officer may, for determining the value of the property provision-
ally attached under sub-section (1), make a reference to the Valuation Officer, who
shall estimate the fair market value of the property in the manner provided under
section 269(3) to (7), and submit a report of such estimate to the Assessing Officer
within thirty days from the date of receipt of the reference.
(7) An order revoking the provisional attachment under sub-section (4) or (5) shall
be made—
       (a) within forty-five days from the date of receipt of the guarantee, where a
           reference to the Valuation Officer has been made under sub-section (6);
           or
       (b) within fifteen days from the date of receipt of guarantee, in any other
           case.
(8) Where a notice of demand specifying a sum payable is served upon the asses-
see and the assessee fails to pay that sum within the time specified, the Assessing
Officer may invoke the guarantee furnished under sub-section (4) or (5), wholly or
in part, to recover the amount.
(9) The Assessing Officer shall, in the interests of revenue, invoke the bank guaran-
tee, if the assessee fails to renew the guarantee referred to in sub-section (4) or (5),
or fails to furnish a new guarantee from a scheduled bank for an equal amount,
before fifteen days of its expiry.
(10) The amount realised by invoking the guarantee referred to in sub-section (4) or
(5) shall be adjusted against the existing demand which is payable by the assessee;
and the balance amount, if any, shall be deposited in the Personal Deposit Account
of the Principal Commissioner or Commissioner in the branch of, —
       (a) the Reserve Bank of India or the State Bank of India; or
       (b) any bank as may be appointed by the Reserve Bank of India as its agent
           under section 45(1) of the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934 (2 of 1934)
           at the place where the office of the Principal Commissioner or Commis-
           sioner is situated.
(11) Where the Assessing Officer is satisfied that the guarantee referred to in sub-sec-
tion (4) or (5) is not required any more to protect the interests of the revenue, he
shall release that guarantee forthwith.
gold_response:
Section 500 (Provisional attachment to protect revenue in certain cases) refers to Section 269 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

500.		(1) Where, during the pendency of any proceeding for—
       (a) the assessment of any income or for the assessment or reassessment of
           any income, which has escaped assessment; or
       (b) imposition of penalty under section 444, where the amount or aggregate
           of amounts of penalty likely to be imposed under the said section exceeds
           two crore rupees,
the Assessing Officer is of the opinion that for protecting the interests of the reve-
nue it is necessary so to do, he may, with the previous approval of the Competent
Authority by order in writing, attach provisionally any property belonging to the
assessee in the manner prescribed in section 413.
(2) Every provisional attachment under sub-section (1) shall cease to have effect after
the expiry of six months from the date of the order made under the said sub-section.
(3) The Competent Authority may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, extend the
period referred to in sub-section (2) and the total period of such extension shall not
exceed two years or sixty days after the date of order of assessment or reassessment,
whichever is later.

Readers should also refer to Section 269 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
The relationship between Sections 500 and 269 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 is as follows:

Both sections deal with the protection of revenue, but they operate in different contexts.

**Section 500** deals with the provisional attachment of property to protect revenue in certain cases, such as when there is an escape of income or penalty exceeding ₹2 crore.

**Section 269**, on the other hand, deals with the valuation of property for assessing income tax. It provides for estimating the fair market value of property for the purpose of assessing income tax.

In essence, while both sections are related to the protection of revenue, Section 500 focuses on attaching property to prevent evasion of taxes, whereas Section 269 focuses on valuing property for assessing income tax.

To illustrate this difference, consider the following example:

Suppose an assessee escapes ₹50 lakhs in income by using a fake company. In this scenario, Section 500 would attach the property of the fake company to protect the revenue, while Section 269 would require the assessee to provide detailed information about the fake company's activities and assets, and estimate their fair market value to assess the income earned through those activities.

context:
[Section 371 — Amendment of assessment on appeal]
Chapter: APPEALS, REVISIONS AND ALTERNATE DISPUTE RESOLUTIONS

371.		If as a result of an appeal under section 356 or 357 or 362, any change is
					made in the assessment of a body of individuals or an association of persons, or
a new assessment is directed in such cases, the Joint Commissioner (Appeals) or the
Commissioner (Appeals) or the Appellate Tribunal, shall pass an order authorising
the Assessing Officer either to amend the assessment made on any member of the
body or association or to make a fresh assessment on such member.
Exclusion of time taken for copy.
gold_response:
Section 371 (Amendment of assessment on appeal) refers to Section 356 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

371.		If as a result of an appeal under section 356 or 357 or 362, any change is
					made in the assessment of a body of individuals or an association of persons, or
a new assessment is directed in such cases, the Joint Commissioner (Appeals) or the
Commissioner (Appeals) or the Appellate Tribunal, shall pass an order authorising
the Assessing Officer either to amend the assessment made on any member of the
body or association or to make a fresh assessment on such member.
Exclusion of time taken for copy.

Readers should also refer to Section 356 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Sections 371 and 356 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 interact as follows:
- Section 356 allows for revision of assessments if there is an error in the assessment of a person who is assessed under this Act.
- Section 371 allows for amendment of assessments if there is a change in the assessment of a person who is assessed under this Act.

Therefore, sections 356 and 371 work together to allow for revisions and amendments of assessments in certain situations.

instruction: Explain how Section 80 affects the application of Section 139.
context:
[Section 139 — 139.]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

139.		In respect of any tax year, where—
       (a) the gross total income of an assessee, being a Developer, includes any
           profits and gains derived by an undertaking or an enterprise from any
           business of developing a Special Economic Zone, notified on or after the
           1st April, 2005 under the Special Economic Zones Act, 2005 (28 of 2005)
           referred to in section 80-IAB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961);
           and
       (b) such assessee is eligible to claim a deduction from the profits and gains
           derived from such business for such tax year under the provisions of the
           said section, as if the said Act had not been repealed,
there shall be allowed, in computing the total income of the assessee, a deduction
from the profits and gains derived from such business, subject to the conditions
that—
       (i) the amount of deduction is calculated as per the provisions of section
           80-IAB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961); and
      (ii) the deduction under this Act shall be allowed only for such tax years, as
           would have been allowed under section 80-IAB of the Income-tax Act,
           1961 (43 of 1961), as if the said Act had not been repealed.
Special provision in respect of specified business.
gold_response:
Section 139 (139.) refers to Section 80 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

139.		In respect of any tax year, where—
       (a) the gross total income of an assessee, being a Developer, includes any
           profits and gains derived by an undertaking or an enterprise from any
           business of developing a Special Economic Zone, notified on or after the
           1st April, 2005 under the Special Economic Zones Act, 2005 (28 of 2005)
           referred to in section 80-IAB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961);
           and
       (b) such assessee is eligible to claim a deduction from the profits and gains
           derived from such business for such tax year under the provisions of the
           said section, as if the said Act had not been repealed,
there shall be allowed, in computing the total income of the assessee, a deduction
from the profits and gains derived from such business, subject to the conditions
that—
       (i) the amount of deduction is calculated as per the provisions of section
           80-IAB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961); and
      (ii) the deduction under this Act shall be allowed only for such tax years, as
           would have been allowed under section 80-IAB of the Income-tax Act,
           1961

Readers should also refer to Section 80 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 139 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) allows deductions to be made in computing total income when certain conditions are met.

According to Section 139, the gross total income of an assessee that meets two conditions must include profits and gains derived from a specific business. The conditions are:
(i) The profits and gains are derived from a business of developing a Special Economic Zone (SEZ).
(ii) The assessee is eligible to claim a deduction from the profits and gains for the same tax year under the provisions of section 80-IAB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961).

Now, let's analyze how these conditions affect the application of Section 139:

The first condition states that the gross total income of the assessee must include profits and gains derived from a SEZ. This means that the assessee's income will include all the revenue generated from their SEZ activities, including profits and gains from sales, rentals, etc.

The second condition states that the assessee must be eligible to claim a deduction from the profits and gains for the same tax year under section 80-IAB of the Income-tax Act, 1961 (43 of 1961). This implies that the assessee must have incurred profits and gains from their SEZ activities during the same tax year.

Since the SEZ activity is a separate entity from the regular business, it is likely that the SEZ activity will generate its own profits and gains. Therefore, the assessee can claim a deduction for the profits and gains from the SEZ activity without having to deduct the entire gross total income.

However, there is no mention of whether the SEZ activity generates profits and gains that can be claimed as deductions. It is possible that some profits and gains may not be deductible due to various reasons such as depreciation, amortization, etc.

Therefore, the conditions of Section 139 do not cover all cases where profits and gains are derived from a SEZ activity. The assessee may still need to deduct other expenses related to the SEZ activity, such as land acquisition costs, construction costs, etc., which are not mentioned in the given conditions.

instruction: Explain how Section 4 affects the application of Section 317.
context:
[Section 317 — Assessment of persons leaving India]
Chapter: PROCEDURE FOR ASSESSMENT

317.		(1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 4, when it appears to the
					Assessing Officer that any individual may leave India during the current tax
year or shortly after its expiry, with no present intention of returning to India, the
total income of such individual for the period beginning from the first day of that
current tax year up to the probable date of departure from India (referred to as
specified period in this section) shall be chargeable to tax in that current tax year.
(2) The total income of each completed tax year or part of any tax year included in
the specified period shall be chargeable to tax at the rate or rates in force in that
tax year, and separate assessments shall be made in respect of each such completed
tax year or part of any tax year.
(3) The Assessing Officer may estimate the income of such individual for such
specified period or any part thereof, where it cannot be readily determined in the
manner provided in this Act.
(4) For the purposes of making an assessment under sub-section (1), the Assessing
Officer may serve a notice upon such individual requiring him to furnish within
such time, not being less than seven days, as specified in the notice, a return in
the same form and verified in the same manner as a return under section 268(1),
setting forth his—
       (a) total income for each completed tax year comprised in such specified
           period referred to therein; and
       (b) estimated total income for any part of the tax year comprised in such
           specified period,
and the provisions of this Act shall, so far as may be, and subject to the provisions
of this section, apply as if the notice were a notice issued under section 268(1).
(5) Irrespective of anything contained in section 268(1) or 280, where the provisions
of sub-section (1) are applicable, the Assessing Officer may issue any notice under
section 268(1) or 280, requiring the furnishing of the return by such individual in
respect of any tax chargeable under any other provisions of this Act, within such
period, not being less than seven days, as the Assessing Officer may think proper.
(6) The tax chargeable under this section shall be in addition to the tax, if any,
chargeable under any other provisions of this Act.

          10. —Association of persons or body of individuals or artificial
             juridical person formed for a particular event or purpose
Assessment of association of persons or body of individuals or artificial juridical
person formed for a particular event or purpose.
gold_response:
Section 317 (Assessment of persons leaving India) refers to Section 4 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

317.		(1) Irrespective of anything contained in section 4, when it appears to the
					Assessing Officer that any individual may leave India during the current tax
year or shortly after its expiry, with no present intention of returning to India, the
total income of such individual for the period beginning from the first day of that
current tax year up to the probable date of departure from India (referred to as
specified period in this section) shall be chargeable to tax in that current tax year.
(2) The total income of each completed tax year or part of any tax year included in
the specified period shall be chargeable to tax at the rate or rates in force in that
tax year, and separate assessments shall be made in respect of each such completed
tax year or part of any tax year.
(3) The Assessing Officer may estimate the income of such individual for such
specified period or any part thereof, where it cannot be readily determined in the
manner provided in this Act.

Readers should also refer to Section 4 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
According to Section 317, when an individual leaves India during the current tax year or shortly after its expiry, the total income of such individual for the specified period is chargeable to tax in that current tax year. This means that the individual's income is assessed for the entire period they are abroad, regardless of whether they intend to return to India or not.

This provision effectively allows the Assessing Officer to assess the individual's income without waiting until their return to India, which would have been the case under Section 4 of the previous version of the Act. In other words, Section 317 provides a blanket assessment of the individual's income for the specified period, even if they do not intend to return to India.

Therefore, Section 317 does not restrict the Assessing Officer's power to assess the individual's income, but rather extends the assessment period to include the entire period the individual was abroad, allowing for a more comprehensive assessment of their income.

Readers should also refer to Section 124 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 124 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) provides for the creation of a pension scheme for employees of an assessee company. This section allows the assessee to contribute to a pension scheme for their employees.

Now, let's analyze how Section 124 affects the application of Section 29:

**Section 124 creates a pension scheme**: Under Section 124, the assessee can create a pension scheme for their employees, which includes paying out a specified percentage of the employee's salary to them. This is done through a separate account set up for each employee.

**Section 124 does not limit the deductibility of expenses**: Unlike Section 29, which specifically mentions that only certain types of expenses can be deducted under Section 29, Section 124 allows for the creation of a pension scheme without restricting the deductibility of expenses.

In fact, Section 124 explicitly states that "the amount of contribution received from an employee to which the provisions of section 2(49)(o) apply" refers to the specified percentage of the employee's salary paid into the pension scheme. This means that the assessee can pay up to 14% of the employee's salary into the pension scheme, regardless of whether it exceeds the maximum allowed under Section 29.

Therefore, the creation of a pension scheme under Section 124 does not restrict the deductibility of expenses under Section 29, allowing the assessee to claim deductions for these expenses even though they are not directly related to the pension scheme.

Section 275 provides that for certain cases, the draft of the proposed order of assessment can be made up to the time limit specified in the table (Section 61) even if it exceeds the standard time limit of six months.

instruction: Explain how Section 46 affects the application of Section 114.
context:
[Section 114 — Set off and carry forward of losses computed in respect of specified business]
Chapter: SET OFF, OR CARRY FORWARD AND SET OFF OF LOSSES

114. (1) Any loss, computed in respect of a specified business, referred to in section
			 46, shall be set off only against profits and gains of another specified business.
(2) Where for any tax year, loss computed in respect of a specified business cannot
be wholly set off under sub-section (1), so much of the loss not so set off or the whole
loss, as the case may be, shall be carried forward to the following tax year and—
       (i) be set off against the profits and gains, if any, of any specified business
           carried on by him for such tax year; and
       (ii) if the loss cannot be wholly so set off, the amount of loss not so set off
            shall be carried forward to the following tax year and so on.
Set off and carry forward of losses from specified activity.
gold_response:
Section 114 (Set off and carry forward of losses computed in respect of specified business) refers to Section 46 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025. The interaction is as follows:

114. (1) Any loss, computed in respect of a specified business, referred to in section
			 46, shall be set off only against profits and gains of another specified business.
(2) Where for any tax year, loss computed in respect of a specified business cannot
be wholly set off under sub-section (1), so much of the loss not so set off or the whole
loss, as the case may be, shall be carried forward to the following tax year and—
       (i) be set off against the profits and gains, if any, of any specified business
           carried on by him for such tax year; and
       (ii) if the loss cannot be wholly so set off, the amount of loss not so set off
            shall be carried forward to the following tax year and so on.
Set off and carry forward of losses from specified activity.

Readers should also refer to Section 46 for complete understanding of the cross-referenced provisions.
model_response:
Section 114 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) provides for setting off and carrying forward of losses from specified businesses.

According to Section 114, any loss computed in respect of a specified business can be set off against profits and gains of another specified business. However, there is an exception where the loss cannot be wholly set off.

The key point here is that Section 114 allows for the "carry forward" of unsettable losses from one tax year to the next. This means that even if the loss cannot be fully set off in a particular tax year, it can still be carried forward to the subsequent tax years.

To understand this, let's consider an example:

Suppose XYZ Ltd has incurred a loss of ₹10 lakhs in the first tax year due to increased production costs. The company wants to set off this loss against its profits from the same business in the second tax year. However, the loss cannot be fully set off because the company needs to pay out ₹10 lakhs as depreciation expenses.

In this scenario, the company can choose to either:

* Set off the entire loss of ₹10 lakhs against profits from the same business in the second tax year (which would mean paying out ₹10 lakhs in depreciation expenses).
* Carry forward the unsettable loss of ₹10 lakhs to the third tax year and then set off the remaining loss against profits from the same business in the fourth tax year.

This is possible because the unsettable loss can be carried forward to the next tax year without being set off against profits. Therefore, the company can use Section 114 to set off part of the loss against profits from the same business in multiple tax years.

model_response:
Section 71 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) deals with the concept of "capital gains" and "holding companies". According to this section, if a person transfers an asset to a subsidiary company, the entire capital gain is deemed to have been incurred by the parent company.

In the given scenario, the capital gain arises when the transferor sells the asset to the subsidiary company. However, since the entire capital gain is deemed to have been incurred by the parent company, the entire capital gain is also transferred to the subsidiary company.

Therefore, the entire capital gain is not just limited to the specific asset that was sold, but rather the entire capital gain is transferred to the subsidiary company. This means that the entire capital gain is considered as part of the parent company's overall capital gains, regardless of whether the specific asset was sold or not.

This provision effectively eliminates the distinction between the sale of assets and the creation of new shares in the subsidiary company. It implies that the entire capital gain is transferred to the subsidiary company, making it difficult to distinguish between the sale of assets and the creation of new shares.
