model_response:
To comply with Section 106, a taxpayer can follow these steps:
- When borrowing money through a negotiable instrument, hundi, or other modes, they must ensure that the entire amount, including interest, is deducted from their taxable income.
- The interest paid on the borrowed amount will also be included in the taxpayer's taxable income.
- If the borrower is required to pay interest on the borrowed amount, the taxpayer should deduct the interest from their total income.
- In case the borrower is required to repay the amount, the repayment should also include the interest paid on the borrowed amount.
- The taxpayer should keep records of all transactions related to the loan, including the date, amount, and interest paid.
- It is essential to consult with a tax professional or accountant to ensure compliance with the tax laws and regulations.

instruction: How should a taxpayer comply with Section 481 (Failure to comply with a direction of special audit or valuation)?
context:
[Section 481 — Failure to comply with a direction of special audit or valuation]
Chapter: OFFENCES AND PROSECUTION

481.		If a person wilfully fails to comply with a direction issued to him under sec-
						tion 268(5), he shall be punishable with simple imprisonment for a term up to
six months, or with fine, or with both.]
False statement in verification, etc.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 481 (Failure to comply with a direction of special audit or valuation) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

481.		If a person wilfully fails to comply with a direction issued to him under sec-
						tion 268(5), he shall be punishable with simple imprisonment for a term up to
six months, or with fine, or with both.]
False statement in verification, etc.
model_response:
To comply with Section 481, a taxpayer should:

"Obtain a certificate from the auditor that the taxpayer has complied with the direction given to him under section 268(5) of the Income Tax Act, 2025."

This means that taxpayers must obtain a certificate from the auditor stating that they have followed the directions provided under Section 268(5) before proceeding with their tax return. This certificate serves as proof that the taxpayer has fulfilled their obligations under Section 481.

instruction: How should a taxpayer comply with Section 11 (Incomes not included in total income)?
context:
[Section 11 — Incomes not included in total income]
Chapter: INCOMES WHICH DO NOT FORM PART OF TOTAL INCOME

11. (1) In computing the total income of any person for a tax year under this Act,
		 any income enumerated in Schedules II, III, IV, V and VI shall not be included,
subject to fulfilment of conditions specified therein.
(2) Wherever the conditions referred to in the Schedules referred in sub-section (1)
are not satisfied in any tax year in respect of any income enumerated in the said
Schedules, such income shall be charged to tax under this Act on the total income
for that tax year.
(3) The persons enumerated in Schedule VII shall, subject to fulfilment of the
conditions specified therein, not be chargeable to tax under this Act on the total
income for a tax year.
(4) Wherever the conditions referred to in Schedule VII are not satisfied in respect
of the persons enumerated in the said Schedule in any tax year, the income of such
person shall be charged to tax under the provisions of this Act for that tax year.
(5) The Central Government may make rules or issue notifications for the purposes
of this section as specified in Schedules II, III, IV, V, VI and VII.

                  B.—Incomes not to be included in total income
                      of political parties and electoral trusts
Incomes not included in total income of political parties and electoral trusts.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 11 (Incomes not included in total income) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

11. (1) In computing the total income of any person for a tax year under this Act,
		 any income enumerated in Schedules II, III, IV, V and VI shall not be included,
subject to fulfilment of conditions specified therein.
(2) Wherever the conditions referred to in the Schedules referred in sub-section (1)
are not satisfied in any tax year in respect of any income enumerated in the said
Schedules, such income shall be charged to tax under this Act on the total income
for that tax year.
(3) The persons enumerated in Schedule VII shall, subject to fulfilment of the
conditions specified therein, not be chargeable to tax under this Act on the total
income for a tax year.
(4) Wherever the conditions referred to in Schedule VII are not satisfied in respect
of the persons enumerated in the said Schedule in any tax year, the income of such
person shall be charged to tax under the provisions of this Act for that tax year.
(5) The Central Government may make rules or issue notifications for the purposes
of this section as specified in Schedules II, III, IV, V, VI and VII.

                  B.—Incomes not to be included in total income
                      of political parties and electoral trusts
Incomes not included in total income of political parties and electoral trusts.
model_response:
A taxpayer who is required to file their tax return under Section 11 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 must exclude from their total income:
- Income mentioned in Schedules II, III, IV, V, and VI.
However, they do not need to include:
- Income mentioned in Schedule VII unless they satisfy the conditions specified in it.

Therefore, taxpayers can follow these steps to comply with Section 11:

1. Check if the income mentioned in Schedules II, III, IV, V, and VI are excluded from total income.
2. If they are excluded, exclude them from total income.
3. If they are not excluded, check if they satisfy the conditions specified in Schedule VII.
4. If they satisfy the conditions, include their income in total income for the tax year.

instruction: What is the procedure prescribed under Section 80 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025?
context:
[Section 80 — Fair market value deemed to be full value of consideration in certain cases]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

80. If the consideration received or accruing from the transfer of a capital asset
		 is not ascertainable or cannot be determined, its fair market value on the date
of transfer shall be deemed to be the full value of consideration received or accruing
as a result of such transfer for the purposes of computing income under the head
“Capital gains”.
Advance money received.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 80 (Fair market value deemed to be full value of consideration in certain cases) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

80. If the consideration received or accruing from the transfer of a capital asset
		 is not ascertainable or cannot be determined, its fair market value on the date
of transfer shall be deemed to be the full value of consideration received or accruing
as a result of such transfer for the purposes of computing income under the head
“Capital gains”.
Advance money received.
model_response:
The procedure prescribed under Section 80 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 is as follows:
1. The recipient of the capital asset transfers it to another person.
2. The recipient receives advance money from the first person.
3. The fair market value of the capital asset is determined on the date of transfer.
4. The fair market value is then deemed to be the full value of consideration received or accruing as a result of the transfer.

Therefore, the total income is computed by adding up all the incomes that can be directly linked to the capital asset transferred.

instruction: What is the procedure prescribed under Section 275 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025?
context:
[Section 275 — Reference to Dispute Resolution Panel]
Chapter: PROCEDURE FOR ASSESSMENT

275.		(1) The Assessing Officer shall, irrespective of anything to the contrary
					contained in this Act, in the first instance, forward92 a draft of the proposed
order of assessment (hereafter in this section referred to as the draft order) to the
eligible assessee, if he proposes to make any variation which is prejudicial to the
interest of such assessee.
(2) On receipt of the draft order, the eligible assessee shall, within thirty days of its
receipt by him,—
        (a) file his acceptance of the variations to the Assessing Officer; or
        (b) file his objections, if any, to such variation with,—
               (i) the Dispute Resolution Panel; and
              (ii) the Assessing Officer.
(3) The Assessing Officer shall complete the assessment on the basis of the draft
order, if—
        (a) the assessee intimates to the Assessing Officer the acceptance of the
            variation; or
        (b) no objection is received within the period specified in sub-section (2).
 [(4)(a) The Assessing Officer shall, irrespective of anything contained in section
56

286, pass the assessment order under sub-section (3) within one month from the end
of the month in which,—
Officer shall, in conformity with the directions, complete, irrespective of anything to
be an Assessing Officer other than the National Faceless Assessment Centre or any
assessment unit referred to in section 273(3).]
Issue of notice where income has escaped assessment.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 275 (Reference to Dispute Resolution Panel) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

275.		(1) The Assessing Officer shall, irrespective of anything to the contrary
					contained in this Act, in the first instance, forward92 a draft of the proposed
order of assessment (hereafter in this section referred to as the draft order) to the
eligible assessee, if he proposes to make any variation which is prejudicial to the
interest of such assessee.
(2) On receipt of the draft order, the eligible assessee shall, within thirty days of its
receipt by him,—
        (a) file his acceptance of the variations to the Assessing Officer; or
        (b) file his objections, if any, to such variation with,—
               (i) the Dispute Resolution Panel; and
              (ii) the Assessing Officer.
(3) The Assessing Officer shall complete the assessment on the basis of the draft
order, if—
        (a) the assessee intimates to the Assessing Officer the acceptance of the
            variation; or
        (b) no objection is received within the period specified in sub-section (2).
 [(4)(a) The Assessing Officer shall, irrespective of anything contained in section
56

286, pass the assessment order under sub-section (3) within one month from the end
of the month in which,—
Officer shall, in conformity with the directions, complete, irrespective of anything to
be an Assessing Officer other than the National Faceless Assessment Centre or any
assessment unit referred to in section 273(3).]
Issue of notice where income has escaped assessment.
model_response:
The procedure prescribed under Section 275 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 is as follows:
- The Assessing Officer shall forward a draft order of assessment to the eligible assessee.
- The draft order must be sent to the eligible assessee within 30 days of receiving it.
- If the eligible assessee accepts the draft order, they must either accept it or file objections with the Dispute Resolution Panel.
- If the eligible assessee objects to the draft order, they can do so by filing their objections with the Dispute Resolution Panel.
- If the eligible assessee does not object to the draft order within the specified time, the Assessing Officer will proceed with the assessment based on the draft order.

instruction: What is the procedure prescribed under Section 96 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025?
context:
[Section 96 — Transfer of income without transfer of assets]
Chapter: INCOME OF OTHER PERSONS INCLUDED

96. All income arising to any person by virtue of a transfer,—
       (a) whether revocable or not, and whether effected before or after the com-
           mencement of this Act; and
       (b) where there is no transfer of assets from which such income arises,
shall be chargeable to income-tax as the income of the transferor and shall be
included in his total income.
Chargeability of income in transfer of assets.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 96 (Transfer of income without transfer of assets) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

96. All income arising to any person by virtue of a transfer,—
       (a) whether revocable or not, and whether effected before or after the com-
           mencement of this Act; and
       (b) where there is no transfer of assets from which such income arises,
shall be chargeable to income-tax as the income of the transferor and shall be
included in his total income.
Chargeability of income in transfer of assets.
model_response:
According to Section 96 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025, the procedure for charging income to the transferor if there is no transfer of assets is as follows:
The transferor must file an application with the Assessing Officer within 30 days of receiving the notice from the Assessing Officer stating that he has received a notice from the Central Government regarding the transfer of income. The application should include details of the transferor's identity, the date of receipt of the notice, and the amount of income received. If the Assessor does not receive the application within 30 days, the Assessor may issue a notice requiring the transferor to provide additional information.

instruction: Describe the procedural requirements under Section 109.
context:
[Section 109 — Set off of losses under any other head of income]
Chapter: SET OFF, OR CARRY FORWARD AND SET OFF OF LOSSES

109. (1) Subject to the provisions of this Chapter, for any tax year, if income com-
						 puted under any head of income (other than “Capital gains”) is a loss,
such loss shall be set off against income of the assessee under any other head,
including “Capital gains”, if any, assessable for that tax year, subject to the following
conditions:—
       (a) loss under the head “Profits and gains of business or profession” shall
           not be set off against income assessable under the head “Salaries”; and
       (b) loss under the head “Income from house property” shall be set off to the
           extent of ` 200000 against income under any other head.
(2) For any tax year, the loss under the head “Capital gains” shall not be set off
against income under any other head.
Carry forward and set off of loss from house property.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 109 (Set off of losses under any other head of income) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

109. (1) Subject to the provisions of this Chapter, for any tax year, if income com-
						 puted under any head of income (other than “Capital gains”) is a loss,
such loss shall be set off against income of the assessee under any other head,
including “Capital gains”, if any, assessable for that tax year, subject to the following
conditions:—
       (a) loss under the head “Profits and gains of business or profession” shall
           not be set off against income assessable under the head “Salaries”; and
       (b) loss under the head “Income from house property” shall be set off to the
           extent of ` 200000 against income under any other head.
(2) For any tax year, the loss under the head “Capital gains” shall not be set off
against income under any other head.
Carry forward and set off of loss from house property.
model_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 109 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026), as per the given provisions, are as follows:

1. The taxpayer must have incurred a loss under the head "Profits and gains of business or profession" during the tax year.
2. The loss under this head cannot be set off against income assessable under the head "Salaries".
3. The loss under the head "Income from house property" can be set off up to Rs. 2,00,000 against income under any other head.
4. The loss under the head "Capital gains" cannot be set off against income under any other head.

These are the essential procedural requirements specified in Section 109 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026).

instruction: What is the procedure prescribed under Section 325 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025?
context:
[Section 325 — Assessment as a firm]
Chapter: PROCEDURE FOR ASSESSMENT

325.		(1) A firm shall be assessed as a firm for the purposes of this Act, if—
       (a) the partnership is evidenced by an instrument; and
       (b) the individual shares of the partners are specified in that instrument.
(2) A certified copy of the instrument of partnership referred to in sub-section (1)
shall accompany the return of income of the firm of the tax year in respect of which
assessment as a firm is first sought.
(3) For the purposes of sub-section (2), the copy of the instrument of partnership
shall be certified in writing by all the partners (not being minors) or, where the
return is made after the dissolution of the firm, by all persons (not being minors),
who were partners in the firm immediately before its dissolution and by the legal
representative of any such partner who is deceased.
(4) Where a firm is assessed as such for any tax year, it shall be assessed in the same
capacity for every subsequent year, if there is no change in the constitution of the
firm or the shares of the partners as evidenced by the instrument of partnership on
the basis of which the assessment as a firm was first sought.
(5) Where any such change had taken place in the tax year, the firm shall furnish
a certified copy of the revised instrument of partnership along with the return of
income for such tax year, and all the provisions of this section shall apply accordingly.
(6) Irrespective of anything contained in any other provision of this Act, where, in
respect of any tax year, there is on the part of a firm any such failure as is mentioned
in section 271,—
       (a) the firm shall be so assessed that no deduction by way of any payment of
           interest, salary, bonus, commission or remuneration, by whatever name
           called, made by such firm to any partner of such firm shall be allowed
           in computing the income chargeable under the head “Profits and gains
           of business or profession”; and
       (b) such payment shall not be chargeable to income-tax under section
           26(2)(g).
Assessment when section 325 not complied with.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 325 (Assessment as a firm) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

325.		(1) A firm shall be assessed as a firm for the purposes of this Act, if—
       (a) the partnership is evidenced by an instrument; and
       (b) the individual shares of the partners are specified in that instrument.
(2) A certified copy of the instrument of partnership referred to in sub-section (1)
shall accompany the return of income of the firm of the tax year in respect of which
assessment as a firm is first sought.
(3) For the purposes of sub-section (2), the copy of the instrument of partnership
shall be certified in writing by all the partners (not being minors) or, where the
return is made after the dissolution of the firm, by all persons (not being minors),
who were partners in the firm immediately before its dissolution and by the legal
representative of any such partner who is deceased.
(4) Where a firm is assessed as such for any tax year, it shall be assessed in the same
capacity for every subsequent year, if there is no change in the constitution of the
firm or the shares of the partners as evidenced by the instrument of partnership on
the basis of which the assessment as a firm was first sought.
(5) Where any such change had taken place in the tax year, the firm shall furnish
a certified copy of the revised instrument of partnership along with the return of
income for such tax year, and all the provisions of this section shall apply accordingly.
model_response:
The procedure prescribed under Section 325 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 is as follows:
- The firm must have an instrument of partnership evidencing the partnership.
- The instrument must specify the individual shares of the partners.
- The instrument must be certified in writing by all partners (not being minors) or, if the return is made after the dissolution of the firm, by all persons (not being minors) who were partners in the firm immediately before its dissolution.
- If the firm has changed its constitution or shares since the initial assessment, the firm must provide a certified copy of the revised instrument of partnership along with the return of income for the previous tax year.
- In case of changes, the firm must also furnish a certified copy of the revised instrument of partnership along with the return of income for the new tax year.

instruction: How should a taxpayer comply with Section 93 (Deductions)?
context:
[Section 93 — Deductions]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

93. (1) The income chargeable under the head “Income from other sources”
				 shall be computed after making the following deductions:—
   10b
      [(a) for interest on securities, any reasonable sum paid as commission or
           remuneration to a banker or any other person for the purpose of realising
           such interest on behalf of the assessee;]
         (b) for income of the nature referred to in section 92(2)(c), so far as may be,
             an amount as per section 29(1)(e);
         (c) for income of the nature referred to in section 92(2)(f) and (g), so far
             as may be, an amount as per section 28(1)(a), (b), (d), section 33, and
             subject to the provisions of section 28(2);
         (d) for income in the nature of family pension (a regular monthly amount
             payable by the employer to a family member of an employee upon the
             death of such employee),—
               (i) an amount equal to one-third of such income or ` 25000, whichever
                   is less, where income-tax is computed under section 202(1); and
              (ii) an amount equal to one-third of such income or ` 15000, whichever
                   is less, in any other case;

10b. Substituted by the Finance Act, 2026, w.e.f. 1-4-2026. Prior to its substitution, clause (a)
     read as under :
        “(a) for dividends [excluding those referred to in section 2(40)(f)] or interest on securi-
             ties, any reasonable sum paid as commission or remuneration to a banker or any
             other person for the purpose of realising such dividend or interest on behalf of the
             assessee;”
        (e) any other expenditure (not being in the nature of capital expenditure)
            laid out or expended wholly and exclusively for making or earning such
            income;
        (f) for income of the nature referred to in section 92(2)(i), an amount equal
            to 50% of such income and no other deduction shall be allowed under
            this section;
        (g) for income in the nature of commutation of pension received from a
            fund as specified in Schedule VII (Table: Sl. No. 3), the entire amount;
       (h) for income in the nature of gratuity as referred in section 19(2)(g), re-
           ceived on the death of the employee, the entire amount.
  [(2) Irrespective of anything contained in sub-section (1), in respect of any dividend
10c

income or income from units of a Mutual Fund specified under Schedule VII (Table:
Sl. No. 20 or 21) or income from units of a specified company as referred to in section
2(h) of the Unit Trust of India (Transfer of Undertaking and Repeal) Act, 2002 (58 of
2002), no deduction shall be allowed.]
Amounts not deductible.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 93 (Deductions) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

93. (1) The income chargeable under the head “Income from other sources”
				 shall be computed after making the following deductions:—
   10b
      [(a) for interest on securities, any reasonable sum paid as commission or
           remuneration to a banker or any other person for the purpose of realising
           such interest on behalf of the assessee;]
         (b) for income of the nature referred to in section 92(2)(c), so far as may be,
             an amount as per section 29(1)(e);
         (c) for income of the nature referred to in section 92(2)(f) and (g), so far
             as may be, an amount as per section 28(1)(a), (b), (d), section 33, and
             subject to the provisions of section 28(2);
         (d) for income in the nature of family pension (a regular monthly amount
             payable by the employer to a family member of an employee upon the
             death of such employee),—
               (i) an amount equal to one-third of such income or ` 25000, whichever
                   is less, where income-tax is computed under section 202(1); and
              (ii) an amount equal to one-third of such income or ` 15000, whichever
                   is less, in any other case;

10b. Substituted by the Finance Act, 2026, w.e.f. 1-4-2026.
model_response:
To comply with Section 93 (Deductions) of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026), a taxpayer can follow these steps:

1. **Identify the types of income**: Determine which type of income falls under the "Income from other sources" category, as stated in Section 93(1).
2. **Make necessary deductions**:
	* For interest on securities: pay reasonable sums as commission or remuneration to a banker or other person for realising interest on behalf of the assessee.
	* For income of the nature referred to in sections 29(1)(e) and 28(1)(a), (b), (d): pay an amount as per section 29(1)(e) and section 28(1)(a), (b), (d).
	* For income in the nature of family pension: pay an amount equal to one-third of the income or ₹25,000, whichever is less, depending on whether the income is computed under section 202(1) or not.
	* For income in the nature of family pension: pay an amount equal to one-third of the income or ₹15,000, whichever is less, depending on whether the income is computed under section 202(1) or not.
3. **Pay the deducted amounts**: Pay the calculated deductions to the respective persons mentioned in clauses (a), (b), (c), and (d).
4. **Keep records**: Maintain accurate records of all payments made, including receipts, invoices, and payment vouchers.

By following these steps, a taxpayer can ensure compliance with Section 93 (Deductions) of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026).

instruction: How should a taxpayer comply with Section 123 (123.)?
context:
[Section 123 — 123.]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

123.		An individual or a Hindu undivided family, shall be allowed a deduction
				of the whole of the amount paid or deposited in the tax year, being the aggre-
gate of the sums enumerated in Schedule XV, as does not exceed ` 150000, while
computing the total income for that year, subject to the conditions specified in that
Schedule.
Deduction in respect of employer and assessee contribution to pension
scheme of Central Government.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 123 (123.) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

123.		An individual or a Hindu undivided family, shall be allowed a deduction
				of the whole of the amount paid or deposited in the tax year, being the aggre-
gate of the sums enumerated in Schedule XV, as does not exceed ` 150000, while
computing the total income for that year, subject to the conditions specified in that
Schedule.
Deduction in respect of employer and assessee contribution to pension
scheme of Central Government.
model_response:
To comply with Section 123, an individual or a Hindu undivided family should make a "contribution" to their Pension Scheme under Section 80(1) of the Income Tax Act, 2025.

This means they should contribute to a pension scheme through a pension plan, such as a NPS (National Pension System), a PPF (Public Provident Fund), or any other approved pension scheme. The contributions made towards this scheme can then be claimed as deductions from taxable income under Section 80(1) of the Act.

instruction: What is the procedure prescribed under Section 230 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025?
context:
[Section 230 — Exclusion of deduction, loss, set off, etc]
Chapter: DETERMINATION OF TAX IN SPECIAL CASES

230. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in any other provision of this Act, in
			 computing the tonnage income of a tonnage tax company for any tax year
(herein referred to as the “relevant tax year”) in which it is chargeable to tax as per
this Part—
       (a) sections 28 to 52 shall apply as if every loss, allowance or deduction
           referred to therein and relating to or allowable for any of the relevant
           tax years, had been given full effect to for that tax year itself;
       (b) no loss referred to in section 108(1) or (2)(b) or 109(1) or 112(1) or 116(1),
           in so far as such loss relates to the business of operating qualifying ships
           of the company, shall be carried forward or set off where such loss re-
           lates to any of the tax years when the company is under the tonnage tax
           scheme;
       (c) no deduction shall be allowed under Chapter VIII in relation to the profits
           and gains from the business of operating qualifying ships; and
       (d) in computing the depreciation allowance under section 33, the written
           down value of any asset used for the purposes of the tonnage tax business
           shall be computed as if the company has claimed and has been actually
           allowed the deduction in respect of depreciation for the relevant tax
           years.
(2) Section 112 shall apply in respect of any losses that have accrued to a company
before its option for tonnage tax scheme and which are attributable to its tonnage
tax business, as if such losses had been set off against the relevant shipping income
in any of the tax years when the company is under the tonnage tax scheme.
(3) The losses referred to in sub-section (2) shall not be available for set off against
any income other than relevant shipping income in any tax year beginning on or
after the company exercises its option under section 231.
(4) Any apportionment necessary to determine the losses referred to in sub-section
(2) shall be made on a reasonable basis.
Method of opting of tonnage tax scheme and validity.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 230 (Exclusion of deduction, loss, set off, etc) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

230. (1) Irrespective of anything contained in any other provision of this Act, in
			 computing the tonnage income of a tonnage tax company for any tax year
(herein referred to as the “relevant tax year”) in which it is chargeable to tax as per
this Part—
       (a) sections 28 to 52 shall apply as if every loss, allowance or deduction
           referred to therein and relating to or allowable for any of the relevant
           tax years, had been given full effect to for that tax year itself;
       (b) no loss referred to in section 108(1) or (2)(b) or 109(1) or 112(1) or 116(1),
           in so far as such loss relates to the business of operating qualifying ships
           of the company, shall be carried forward or set off where such loss re-
           lates to any of the tax years when the company is under the tonnage tax
           scheme;
       (c) no deduction shall be allowed under Chapter VIII in relation to the profits
           and gains from the business of operating qualifying ships; and
       (d) in computing the depreciation allowance under section 33, the written
           down value of any asset used for the purposes of the tonnage tax business
           shall be computed as if the company has claimed and has been actually
           allowed the deduction in respect of depreciation for the relevant tax
           years.
model_response:
The procedure prescribed under Section 230 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 is as follows:

1. In computing the tonnage income of a tonnage tax company for any tax year, sections 28 to 52 apply as if every loss, allowance or deduction referred to in those sections had been given full effect to for that tax year itself.

2. No loss referred to in sections 108, 109, 112, or 116 of the Act shall be carried forward or set off where such loss relates to the business of operating qualifying ships of the company.

3. No deduction shall be allowed under Chapter VIII in relation to the profits and gains from the business of operating qualifying ships.

4. In computing the depreciation allowance under section 33, the written-down value of any asset used for the purposes of the tonnage tax business shall be computed as if the company has claimed and has been actually allowed the deduction in respect of depreciation for the relevant tax years.

5. Method of opting for tonnage tax scheme and validity: The method of opting for tonnage tax scheme is specified in section 231 of the Act, but the validity of this opt-out is contingent upon the exercise of the option under section 231.

instruction: Describe the procedural requirements under Section 522.
context:
[Section 522 — No return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other proceed-
					ings relating thereto, furnished or made or issu]
Chapter: MISCELLANEOUS

522.		(1) No return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other proceed-
					ings relating thereto, furnished or made or issued or taken, or purported to have
been furnished or made or issued or taken, in pursuance of any of the provisions of this
Act, shall be invalid or shall be deemed to be invalid merely by reason of any mistake,
defect or omission in such return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other
proceeding, if such return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other proceeding
is in substance and effect in conformity with or according to the intent and purposes
of this Act.
(2) No assessment under any of the provisions of this Act shall be invalid on the
ground of any mistake, defect or omission in respect of quoting of a computer gener-
ated Document Identification Number, if the assessment order is referenced by such
number in any manner.
(3) Irrespective of anything contained in this Act, any approval given by an income-tax
authority in relation to any assessment, reassessment or recomputation proceedings
shall be deemed to be administrative and supervisory in nature and, shall not be invalid
by reason of any insufficiency of the reasons recorded or by reason of any defect in
the form or manner of its authentication or communication including whether digital
signature have been appended to such approval or not, where such approval has been
granted electronically.]
Notice deemed to be valid in certain circumstances.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 522 (No return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other proceed-
					ings relating thereto, furnished or made or issu) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

522.		(1) No return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other proceed-
					ings relating thereto, furnished or made or issued or taken, or purported to have
been furnished or made or issued or taken, in pursuance of any of the provisions of this
Act, shall be invalid or shall be deemed to be invalid merely by reason of any mistake,
defect or omission in such return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other
proceeding, if such return of income, assessment, notice, summons or other proceeding
is in substance and effect in conformity with or according to the intent and purposes
of this Act.
(2) No assessment under any of the provisions of this Act shall be invalid on the
ground of any mistake, defect or omission in respect of quoting of a computer gener-
ated Document Identification Number, if the assessment order is referenced by such
number in any manner.
(3) Irrespective of anything contained in this Act, any approval given by an income-tax
authority in relation to any assessment, reassessment or recomputation proceedings
shall be deemed to be administrative and supervisory in nature and, shall not be invalid
by reason of any insufficiency of the reasons recorded or by reason of any defect in
the form or manner of its authentication or communication including whether digital
signature have been appended to such approval or not, where such approval has been
granted electronically.]
Notice deemed to be valid in certain circumstances.
model_response:
According to Section 522 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026), the following are the procedural requirements for returns of income, assessments, notices, summons, or other proceeding that are furnished or made or taken or purported to have been furnished or made or taken in pursuance of any provision of this Act:

1. The return must be furnished or made or taken or purported to have been furnished or made or taken in pursuance of any provision of this Act.

This means that the taxpayer must submit their return within the specified timeframe, which is usually 30 days from the date of issue of the notice or summons.

2. The return should be furnished or made or taken or purported to have been furnished or made or taken in pursuance of any provision of this Act.

This implies that the taxpayer must provide all necessary information and documents related to their income, even if it's just a summary or a rough estimate.

3. The return should be furnished or made or taken or purported to have been furnished or made or taken in pursuance of any provision of this Act.

This means that the taxpayer must ensure that they comply with all aspects of the tax laws, including providing all required documentation and information.

4. The return should be furnished or made or taken or purported to have been furnished or made or taken in pursuance of any provision of this Act.

This requirement applies to all types of returns, including individual returns, business returns, and self-assessments.

5. The return should be furnished or made or taken or purported to have been furnished or made or taken in pursuance of any provision of this Act.

This includes ensuring that the return is accurate, complete, and true to the best of one's ability.

In summary, the procedural requirements under Section 522 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) require taxpayers to furnish or make or take or purported to have been furnished or made or taken returns in accordance with the specific provisions of the Act.

instruction: How should a taxpayer comply with Section 434 (Refund for denying liability to deduct tax in certain cases)?
context:
[Section 434 — Refund for denying liability to deduct tax in certain cases]
Chapter: REFUNDS

434.		(1) Where,—
       (a) under an agreement or other arrangement, in writing, the tax deductible
           on any income, other than interest in section 393(2) (Table: Sl. No. 17),
           is to be borne by the person by whom the income is payable; and
       (b) such person having paid such tax to the credit of the Central Government
           claims that no tax was required to be deducted on such income,
he may, within thirty days from the date of payment of such tax, file an application
before the Assessing Officer for refund of such tax in such form and such manner,
as may be prescribed.
(2) The Assessing Officer shall, by an order in writing, allow or reject the application
as referred to in sub-section (1).
(3) No application under sub-section (1) shall be rejected unless an opportunity of
being heard has been given to the applicant.
(4) The Assessing Officer may, before passing an order under sub-section (2), make
such inquiry as he considers necessary.
(5) The order under sub-section (2) shall be passed within six months from the end
of the month in which application under sub-section (1) is received.
Refund on appeal, etc.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 434 (Refund for denying liability to deduct tax in certain cases) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

434.		(1) Where,—
       (a) under an agreement or other arrangement, in writing, the tax deductible
           on any income, other than interest in section 393(2) (Table: Sl. No. 17),
           is to be borne by the person by whom the income is payable; and
       (b) such person having paid such tax to the credit of the Central Government
           claims that no tax was required to be deducted on such income,
he may, within thirty days from the date of payment of such tax, file an application
before the Assessing Officer for refund of such tax in such form and such manner,
as may be prescribed.
(2) The Assessing Officer shall, by an order in writing, allow or reject the application
as referred to in sub-section (1).
(3) No application under sub-section (1) shall be rejected unless an opportunity of
being heard has been given to the applicant.
(4) The Assessing Officer may, before passing an order under sub-section (2), make
such inquiry as he considers necessary.
(5) The order under sub-section (2) shall be passed within six months from the end
of the month in which application under sub-section (1) is received.
Refund on appeal, etc.
model_response:
To comply with Section 434 of the Income Tax Act, 2025, a taxpayer should follow these steps:

1. Ensure that they have a written agreement or arrangement with the payer where the tax deductible amount is specified, including the type of income and the amount of tax to be deducted.
2. Pay the tax to the credit of the Central Government within 30 days from the date of payment.
3. File an application for refund within 30 days from the date of payment, specifying the amount of tax to be refunded.
4. Submit the application in the prescribed form and manner.
5. If the application is rejected, request an opportunity of being heard.
6. The Assessing Officer will then investigate the matter and pass an order allowing or rejecting the application.
7. The order will be passed within six months from the end of the month in which the application is received.

By following these steps, a taxpayer can ensure compliance with Section 434 of the Income Tax Act, 2025.

instruction: How should a taxpayer comply with Section 214 (Tax on investment income and long-term capital gains)?
context:
[Section 214 — Tax on investment income and long-term capital gains]
Chapter: DETERMINATION OF TAX IN SPECIAL CASES

214.		 The income-tax payable on the total income of an assessee, being a non-
				 resident Indian, which includes income specified in column B of the Table
below, shall be the aggregate of income-tax computed at the rate specified in the
column C applied on the corresponding income specified in column B.
                                      TABLE

 Sl.                           Income                              Rate of Income-
 No.                                                                 tax payable
  A                               B                                       C
 1.    Income from investment.                                    20%
 2.    Income from long-term capital gains on specified asset. 12.5%
 3.    Total income as reduced by income referred to against Rates in force.
       serial numbers 1 and 2.
Capital gains on transfer of foreign exchange assets not to be charged in
certain cases.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 214 (Tax on investment income and long-term capital gains) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

214.		 The income-tax payable on the total income of an assessee, being a non-
				 resident Indian, which includes income specified in column B of the Table
below, shall be the aggregate of income-tax computed at the rate specified in the
column C applied on the corresponding income specified in column B.
                                      TABLE

 Sl.                           Income                              Rate of Income-
 No.                                                                 tax payable
  A                               B                                       C
 1.    Income from investment.                                    20%
 2.    Income from long-term capital gains on specified asset. 12.5%
 3.    Total income as reduced by income referred to against Rates in force.
       serial numbers 1 and 2.
Capital gains on transfer of foreign exchange assets not to be charged in
certain cases.
model_response:
A taxpayer should calculate the total income of the non-resident Indian by adding up the income from investments and long-term capital gains, and then apply the tax rate specified in column C to this sum.

For example, if the taxpayer's total income is Rs. 100,000, they would add up the income from investments (Rs. 50,000) and long-term capital gains (Rs. 25,000), resulting in a total of Rs. 75,000. They would then apply the tax rate specified in column C (12.5%) to this sum, which would result in a tax liability of Rs. 9,375 (12.5% of Rs. 75,000).

Therefore, the taxpayer would pay Rs. 9,375 in tax under Section 214.

instruction: Describe the procedural requirements under Section 474.
context:
[Section 474 — 474.]
Chapter: OFFENCES AND PROSECUTION

474.		If a person, who is required to afford the authorised officer with the necessary
					facility to inspect the books of account or other documents, under section
247(1)(ii)15, fails to do so, he shall be punishable with 21[simple imprisonment for a
term up to six months, or with fine, or with both].
Removal, concealment, transfer or delivery of property to prevent tax recovery.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 474 (474.) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

474.		If a person, who is required to afford the authorised officer with the necessary
					facility to inspect the books of account or other documents, under section
247(1)(ii)15, fails to do so, he shall be punishable with 21[simple imprisonment for a
term up to six months, or with fine, or with both].
Removal, concealment, transfer or delivery of property to prevent tax recovery.
model_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 474 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) are as follows:

1. The accused person must be required to afford the authorized officer with the necessary facility to inspect the books of account or other documents.

2. This requirement can be fulfilled through various means such as:
- Providing access to the books of account or documents within a reasonable time.
- Permitting inspection at a reasonable hour.
- Allowing inspection without any obstruction or hindrance.

3. If the accused person fails to comply with this requirement, they may face penalties including imprisonment for a term up to six months, or a fine, or both.

instruction: How should a taxpayer comply with Section 105 (Unexplained expenditure)?
context:
[Section 105 — Unexplained expenditure]
Chapter: AGGREGATION OF INCOME

105.		 (1) Where any expenditure has been incurred by the assessee in any tax
				year, and—
       (a) the assessee offers no explanation about the source of such expenditure
           or part thereof; or
       (b) the explanation offered about the source of such expenditure by the
           assessee is not satisfactory in the opinion of the Assessing Officer,
then, the amount covered by such expenditure or part thereof, shall be deemed to
be the income of the assessee for that tax year.
(2) Irrespective of any other provision of this Act, the amount deemed as income in
sub-section (1) shall not be allowed as a deduction under this Act.
Amount borrowed or repaid through negotiable instrument, hundi, etc.
gold_response:

           or part thereof; or
       (b) the explanation offered about the source of such expenditure by the
           assessee is not satisfactory in the opinion of the Assessing Officer,
then, the amount covered by such expenditure or part thereof, shall be deemed to
be the income of the assessee for that tax year.
(2) Irrespective of any other provision of this Act, the amount deemed as income in
sub-section (1) shall not be allowed as a deduction under this Act.
Amount borrowed or repaid through negotiable instrument, hundi, etc.
model_response:
To comply with Section 105 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026), a taxpayer should provide an explanation for unexplained expenditures within the specified timeframe.

The taxpayer should offer an explanation for the source of the expenditure, even if it's not satisfactory from the Assessing Officer's perspective. This can be done within 30 days of receiving the notice from the Assessing Officer. The explanation must be provided in writing and should include details about the nature of the expenditure, its purpose, and how it relates to the business or profession of the taxpayer.

instruction: What is the procedure prescribed under Section 213 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025?
context:
[Section 213 — Special provision for computation of total income of non-residents]
Chapter: DETERMINATION OF TAX IN SPECIAL CASES

213.		(1) No deduction in respect of any expenditure or allowance shall be allowed
					under any provision of this Act in computing the investment income of a
non-resident Indian.
(2) In the case of an assessee, being a non-resident Indian, where—
       (a) the gross total income consists only of investment income or income
           by way of long-term capital gains or both, then no deduction shall be
           allowed under Chapter VIII;
       (b) the gross total income includes any income referred to in clause (a),—
             (i) the gross total income shall be reduced by such income; and
            (ii) the deductions under Chapter VIII shall be allowed as if the gross
                 total income as so reduced was the gross total income of the asses-
                 see.
Tax on investment income and long-term capital gains.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 213 (Special provision for computation of total income of non-residents) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

213.		(1) No deduction in respect of any expenditure or allowance shall be allowed
					under any provision of this Act in computing the investment income of a
non-resident Indian.
(2) In the case of an assessee, being a non-resident Indian, where—
       (a) the gross total income consists only of investment income or income
           by way of long-term capital gains or both, then no deduction shall be
           allowed under Chapter VIII;
       (b) the gross total income includes any income referred to in clause (a),—
             (i) the gross total income shall be reduced by such income; and
            (ii) the deductions under Chapter VIII shall be allowed as if the gross
                 total income as so reduced was the gross total income of the asses-
                 see.
Tax on investment income and long-term capital gains.
model_response:
The procedure prescribed under Section 213 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025 is as follows:
- The gross total income of a non-resident Indian (NRI) is computed by reducing the gross total income from other sources by the specified income.
- If the gross total income from other sources is less than the specified income, the deductions under Chapter VIII are not allowed.

instruction: Describe the procedural requirements under Section 409.
context:
[Section 409 — When assessee is deemed to be in default]
Chapter: APPEALS, REVISIONS AND ALTERNATE DISPUTE RESOLUTIONS

409.		A person shall be deemed to be an assessee in default, if such person—
       (a) does not pay on the date specified in section 408, any instalment of the
           advance tax that he is required to pay by an order of the Assessing Officer
           under section 407(1) and (4); or
       (b) does not send to the Assessing Officer an intimation under section
           407(8) on or before the date on which any such instalment as is not paid
           becomes due; or
       (c) does not pay on the basis of his estimate of his current income, the ad-
           vance tax payable by him under section 407(9),
in respect of such instalment or instalments.
Credit for advance tax.
gold_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 409 (When assessee is deemed to be in default) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

409.		A person shall be deemed to be an assessee in default, if such person—
       (a) does not pay on the date specified in section 408, any instalment of the
           advance tax that he is required to pay by an order of the Assessing Officer
           under section 407(1) and (4); or
       (b) does not send to the Assessing Officer an intimation under section
           407(8) on or before the date on which any such instalment as is not paid
           becomes due; or
       (c) does not pay on the basis of his estimate of his current income, the ad-
           vance tax payable by him under section 407(9),
in respect of such instalment or instalments.
Credit for advance tax.
model_response:
The procedural requirements under Section 409 of the Income Tax Act, 2025 (as amended by the Finance Act, 2026) are as follows:

(a) The assessment of default:
- A person is considered to be in default if they fail to pay the instalment of advance tax within the specified time period.

(b) Notification requirement:
- The person must send an intimation to the Assessing Officer about the non-payment of the instalment of advance tax.

(c) Payment of advance tax:
- If the person fails to pay the estimated amount of advance tax, they must pay it according to their actual income.

instruction: Who is eligible to claim a deduction under Section 181?
context:
[Section 181 — If an arrangement is declared to be an impermissible avoidance
					arrangement, then, the consequences, in relation to ]
Chapter: GENERAL ANTI-AVOIDANCE RULE

181.		(1) If an arrangement is declared to be an impermissible avoidance
					arrangement, then, the consequences, in relation to tax, of the arrangement,
including denial of tax benefit or a benefit under a tax treaty, shall be determined,
in the manner as deemed appropriate in the circumstances of the case.
(2) The consequences of an arrangement declared to be an impermissible avoidance
arrangement as referred to in sub-section (1) shall include but shall not be limited
to the following:—
       (a) disregarding, combining or recharacterising any step in, or a part or
           whole of, the impermissible avoidance arrangement;
       (b) treating the impermissible avoidance arrangement as if it had not been
           entered into or carried out;
       (c) disregarding any accommodating party or treating any accommodating
           party and any other party as one and the same person;
       (d) deeming persons who are connected persons in relation to each other to
           be one and the same person for the purposes of determining tax treatment
           of any amount;
       (e) reallocating amongst the parties to the arrangement—
             (i) any accrual, or receipt, of a capital nature or revenue nature; or
            (ii) any expenditure, deduction, relief or rebate;
       (f) treating—
             (i) the place of residence of any party to the arrangement; or
            (ii) the situs of an asset or of a transaction,
		 at a place other than the place of residence, location of the asset or
   location of the transaction as provided under the arrangement; or
       (g) considering or looking through any arrangement by disregarding any
           corporate structure.
(3) In this section,—
       (a) any equity may be treated as debt or vice versa;
      (b) any accrual, or receipt, of a capital nature may be treated as of revenue
          nature or vice versa; or
       (c) any expenditure, deduction, relief or rebate may be recharacterised.
Treatment of connected person and accommodating party.

					arrangement, then, the consequences, in relation to ) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

including denial of tax benefit or a benefit under a tax treaty, shall be determined,
in the manner as deemed appropriate in the circumstances of the case.

arrangement as referred to in sub-section (1) shall include but shall not be limited
to the following:—

       (b) treating the impermissible avoidance arrangement as if it had not been
           entered into or carried out;
       (c) disregarding any accommodating party or treating any accommodating
           party and any other party as one and the same person;
       (d) deeming persons who are connected persons in relation to each other to
           be one and the same person for the purposes of determining tax treatment
           of any amount;
       (e) reallocating amongst the parties to the arrangement—
             (i) any accrual, or receipt, of a capital nature or revenue nature; or
            (ii) any expenditure, deduction, relief or rebate;
       (f) treating—
             (i) the place of residence of any party to the arrangement; or
            (ii) the situs of an asset or of a transaction,
		 at a place other than the place of residence, locatio

Eligibility and conditions must be read with any relevant provisos and sub-sections.
model_response:
According to the given provision, the following individuals are eligible to claim a deduction under Section 181:
(a) A person who is connected with another person in relation to whom the arrangement has been declared to be an impermissible avoidance arrangement.

(b) An accommodating party is defined as a person who treats any other party as one and the same person.

instruction: Can a senior citizen claim a deduction under Section 134 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025?
context:
[Section 134 — Deductions in respect of rents paid]
Chapter: DEDUCTIONS TO BE MADE IN COMPUTING TOTAL INCOME

134.			(1) In computing the total income of an assessee, subject to other provisions
						of this section, there shall be deducted any expenditure incurred by him
towards payment of rent (by whatever name called) in respect of any furnished or
unfurnished accommodation occupied by him for the purposes of his own residence.
(2) The deduction under sub-section (1) shall be allowable on payment of such rent
exceeding 10% of his total income, subject to a maximum of ` 5000 per month, or
25% of total income for tax year, whichever is less.
(3) For the purposes of deduction under sub-section (1), such other conditions
or limitations having regard to the area or place in which such accommodation
is situated and other relevant consideration, as may be prescribed, shall be taken
into account.
(4) No deduction under this section shall be allowed to an assessee in any case,
where—
      (a) any residential accommodation is—
             (i) owned by the assessee or by his spouse or minor child or, where
                 such assessee is a member of a Hindu undivided family, by such
                 family at the place where he ordinarily resides or performs duties
                 of his office or employment or carries on his business or profession;
                 or
            (ii) owned by the assessee at any other place, being accommodation
                 in the occupation of the assessee, the value of which is to be deter-
                 mined under section 21(6) or (7)(a); or
       (b) the assessee has any income falling in Schedule III (Table: Sl. No. 11).
(5) For the purposes of this section, the expressions “10% of his total income” and
“25% of his total income” shall mean 10% or 25%, as the case may be, of the total
income of the assessee before allowing deduction for any expenditure under this
section.
Deduction in respect of certain donations for scientific research or rural
development.
gold_response:
Under Section 134 (Deductions in respect of rents paid) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

134.			(1) In computing the total income of an assessee, subject to other provisions
						of this section, there shall be deducted any expenditure incurred by him
towards payment of rent (by whatever name called) in respect of any furnished or
unfurnished accommodation occupied by him for the purposes of his own residence.
(2) The deduction under sub-section (1) shall be allowable on payment of such rent
exceeding 10% of his total income, subject to a maximum of ` 5000 per month, or
25% of total income for tax year, whichever is less.
(3) For the purposes of deduction under sub-section (1), such other conditions
or limitations having regard to the area or place in which such accommodation
is situated and other relevant consideration, as may be prescribed, shall be taken
into account.

Eligibility and conditions must be read with any relevant provisos and sub-sections.
model_response:
No, according to the given provision, a senior citizen cannot claim a deduction under Section 134 of the Income-Tax Act, 2025.

The provision states that "For the purposes of deduction under sub-section (1), such other conditions or limitations having regard to the area or place in which such accommodation is situated and other relevant consideration, as may be prescribed, shall be taken into account." 

It specifically excludes seniors from claiming deductions under this section if they have any income falling in Schedule III (Table: Sl. No. 11). This means that a senior citizen's income does not fall within the specified category, so they cannot claim a deduction under Section 134.

instruction: Who is eligible to claim a deduction under Section 49?
context:
[Section 49 — Site Restoration Fund]
Chapter: COMPUTATION OF TOTAL INCOME

49. (1) An assessee carrying on a business of prospecting, extracting, or producing
		 petroleum or natural gas, or both, in India, and who has an agreement with
the Central Government for this business, shall be allowed a deduction on the basis
of deposit to special account or site restoration account and computed as per the
provisions of the Schedule X.
(2) Any amount withdrawn or transferred from the aforesaid accounts at the time
of closure or otherwise shall be charged to tax in the year in which the amount is
transferred or withdrawn as per the provisions of the Schedule X.
(3) Where any asset acquired as per the special scheme, or the deposit scheme, as
referred to in Schedule X, is sold or otherwise transferred in any tax year, it shall
be charged to tax in accordance with the provisions of the said Schedule.
Special provision in case of trade, profession or similar association.
gold_response:
Under Section 49 (Site Restoration Fund) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

49. (1) An assessee carrying on a business of prospecting, extracting, or producing
		 petroleum or natural gas, or both, in India, and who has an agreement with
the Central Government for this business, shall be allowed a deduction on the basis
of deposit to special account or site restoration account and computed as per the
provisions of the Schedule X.
(2) Any amount withdrawn or transferred from the aforesaid accounts at the time
of closure or otherwise shall be charged to tax in the year in which the amount is

(3) Where any asset acquired as per the special scheme, or the deposit scheme, as
referred to in Schedule X, is sold or otherwise transferred in any tax year, it shall

Special provision in case of trade, profession or similar association.

Eligibility and conditions must be read with any relevant provisos and sub-sections.
model_response:
According to Section 49 of the Income Tax Act, 2025, the following individuals are eligible to claim a deduction:
(i) The assessee carrying on a business of prospecting, extracting, or producing petroleum or natural gas, or both, in India;
(ii) The assessee who has an agreement with the Central Government for this business.

instruction: Who is eligible to claim a deduction under Section 213?
context:
[Section 213 — Special provision for computation of total income of non-residents]
Chapter: DETERMINATION OF TAX IN SPECIAL CASES

213.		(1) No deduction in respect of any expenditure or allowance shall be allowed
					under any provision of this Act in computing the investment income of a
non-resident Indian.
(2) In the case of an assessee, being a non-resident Indian, where—
       (a) the gross total income consists only of investment income or income
           by way of long-term capital gains or both, then no deduction shall be
           allowed under Chapter VIII;
       (b) the gross total income includes any income referred to in clause (a),—
             (i) the gross total income shall be reduced by such income; and
            (ii) the deductions under Chapter VIII shall be allowed as if the gross
                 total income as so reduced was the gross total income of the asses-
                 see.
Tax on investment income and long-term capital gains.
gold_response:
Under Section 213 (Special provision for computation of total income of non-residents) of the Income-Tax Act, 2025:

213.		(1) No deduction in respect of any expenditure or allowance shall be allowed
					under any provision of this Act in computing the investment income of a
non-resident Indian.
(2) In the case of an assessee, being a non-resident Indian, where—
       (a) the gross total income consists only of investment income or income
           by way of long-term capital gains or both, then no deduction shall be
           allowed under Chapter VIII;
       (b) the gross total income includes any income referred to in clause (a),—
             (i) the gross total income shall be reduced by such income; and
            (ii) the deductions under Chapter VIII shall be allowed as if the gross
                 total income as so reduced was the gross total income of the asses-
                 see.
Tax on investment income and long-term capital gains.

Eligibility and conditions must be read with any relevant provisos and sub-sections.
model_response:
According to Section 213, the following individuals are eligible to claim a deduction:
(a) The gross total income of the individual consists only of investment income or income by way of long-term capital gains or both.

(b) The gross total income includes any income referred to in clause (a), 
(i) the gross total income shall be reduced by such income; 
(ii) the deductions under Chapter VIII shall be allowed as if the gross total income as so reduced was the gross total income of the assessee.
